translation: Ta, Ha
commentary:
طه

The surah opens with these mysterious letters: Ta-Ha. These are among the disjointed letters (huruf muqatta'at) that appear at the beginning of several surahs. While we cannot claim definitive knowledge of their complete meaning, scholars have noted an interesting pattern: every surah beginning with the letters Ta-Ha contains a narrative involving Musa (AS) and a snake. Some scholars have observed that the letter ط  in Arabic script resembles a coiled snake standing upright—the coil at the bottom and the vertical portion like a raised serpent. Whether this visual connection holds deeper significance, Allah ﷻ knows best, but it remains a fascinating observation.

translation: We have not sent down to you the Qur'an that you be distressed
commentary:
مَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لِتَشْقَىٰٓ

We did not send down the Quran upon you so that you would be distressed.

This ayah begins with a profound reassurance to the Prophet ﷺ. The choice of words here is deliberate and meaningful. Allah ﷻ uses the expression أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكَ, which conveys the sense of something being placed upon you as a responsibility or burden. If the Quran were merely being described as a gift, the Arabic would use a different preposition. But here, the word "upon" acknowledges that this revelation does indeed come with weight and responsibility.

The word لِتَشْقَىٰٓ carries multiple layers of meaning. At its surface, it refers to extreme exhaustion, being overwhelmed, or overworked. We must understand the context: from the moment the Quran began descending upon the Prophet ﷺ, his life transformed dramatically. The ease and quiet he once knew disappeared, replaced by mounting pressures from every direction. The people of Makkah challenged him constantly, attacked him verbally and physically, and subjected him to increasing hostility. Meanwhile, the responsibility of delivering this message weighed heavily upon him.

But there's another dimension to the word شقاء. Its root letters connect to another word meaning failure. Allah ﷻ is telling His Messenger ﷺ: I did not send down this Quran upon you to see you fail. Your success is guaranteed. Don't be depressed thinking this message won't prevail over all other religions and powers around you.

This reassurance was crucial for the Prophet ﷺ in Makkah, where the situation appeared hopeless from a human perspective. There was no logical reason to believe circumstances would improve. Even the attempted migration to Ta'if had failed miserably. When the eventual migration to Madinah did occur, the Prophet ﷺ and Abu Bakr (RA) were nearly captured along the way, saved only by Allah's miraculous intervention. As far as human eyes could see, things looked dire and were only deteriorating. Yet Allah ﷻ declares: don't stress about the future. That's not why you were given the Quran.

translation: But only as a reminder for those who fear [Allah]
commentary:
إِلَّا تَذْكِرَةً لِّمَن يَخْشَىٰ

It is only a reminder for whoever fears Allah.

The Quran was given for one essential purpose: as a powerful reminder (تذكرة) for whoever has khashyah—a deep, overwhelming fear of Allah ﷻ.

The word خشية represents a particular type of fear in Arabic—one so powerful it can paralyze a person, stunning them into stillness. The Quran possesses this transformative power: when people with goodness in their hearts genuinely listen to this message, they become stunned with fear. Not terror, but awe. An overwhelming realization of how they've spent their lives, what they've done with their time, whether they've fulfilled their purpose. That moment when you truly feel awake and connected to Allah ﷻ—that's a moment of khashyah.

This ayah contains several profound benefits. First, it reminds us that we cannot know what lies in people's hearts—that knowledge belongs to Allah ﷻ alone. The Messenger ﷺ is instructed to simply keep reminding people, regardless of how they react. Someone who has been your enemy for seven, eight, or ten years might develop the fear of Allah ﷻ overnight. Your job isn't to judge them based on their track record; your job is to continue reminding without becoming depressed if years pass without visible change.

Faith, or even just the seed of khashyah, might exist like a tiny pebble buried somewhere in the ocean of the heart. The Prophet ﷺ is trying to activate it, to find that pearl in the ocean. He might search within a person for years until Allah ﷻ decides it's time for that treasure to be discovered. Some people take their sweet time. Umar (RA) took his time. Hamza (RA) took his time. Even the Prophet's ﷺ own uncle, who supported him throughout his mission, took his time accepting Islam. Not everyone embraces the truth the day it's declared.

Furthermore, by saying لِّمَن يَخْشَىٰ – "for whoever fears" – Allah ﷻ is also telling His Messenger ﷺ: don't worry about those who don't fear Me. Don't be bothered by them. You're going to be surrounded by people who truly fear Allah ﷻ, and they're worth it. These are your companions (Sahaba), and in worldly terms, they will be the greatest treasure you acquire in this life. After the Quran itself, the greatest gift Allah ﷻ gave the Prophet ﷺ was the believers in that Quran—his companions (RA).

translation: A revelation from He who created the earth and highest heavens
commentary:
تَنزِيلًا مِّمَّنْ خَلَقَ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ ٱلْعُلَى

A revelation from He who created the earth and the highest heavens.

This Quran has been sent down gradually (تنزيلاً) from the One who created the earth and the skies—specifically, the highest of skies (السموات العلى).

Two aspects of this ayah deserve attention. First, Allah ﷻ mentions the earth before the skies, which reverses the usual sequence in the Quran. Typically, the heavens are mentioned first. Second, He specifically describes the skies as "the highest" (العلى), which seems redundant since skies are by definition high. Why these two unusual elements?

The answer lies in what this surah will reveal. Much of the narrative will focus on events taking place on earth, particularly the story of Fir'awn (Pharaoh). This tyrant believed himself to be the god of the earth. His people and ancestors claimed divinity over the skies. But Allah ﷻ immediately establishes: I created the earth, and I own the highest skies—heights you cannot even conceive of reaching.

Later in the Quran, Fir'awn is described as someone who "was arrogant in the land" (علا في الأرض). He thought himself high, but Allah ﷻ declares: I own the highest skies, including those you commanded your general to build a tower to reach. This seemingly simple description of Allah's creation sets the stage for understanding the profound contrast between the true King and the false one we'll encounter in this surah.

translation: The Most Merciful [who is] above the Throne established
commentary:
ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱسْتَوَىٰ

The Most Merciful, established upon the Throne.

After describing His creation, Allah ﷻ identifies Himself with a beautiful name: الرحمن – the Extremely, Unimaginably Merciful. This King is fundamentally different from the king whose story will unfold in this surah. While Fir'awn ruled with tyranny and cruelty, the true King rules with boundless mercy.

The word استوى carries rich meanings. It conveys the sense of being balanced, taking charge, rising to assume authority. In classical Arabic, this term was used when kings were inaugurated—imagine an old king passing away, and his son, the new king, slowly approaching the throne during an elaborate ceremony. When he finally takes his seat, the soldiers and subjects cheer because that moment marks his official assumption of rule. The word istawa captures that image of stability and established authority.

Allah ﷻ describes Himself as استوى على العرش – established upon the Throne. Where is this Throne? Above the highest skies. The skies themselves soar far above us, but Allah's Throne exists even beyond them. That is where He has established His authority as King and Ruler.

Notice the deliberate structure: Allah ﷻ begins by describing His kingdom from what is closest to us, then moves to what is further above—from earth to the highest skies to the Throne above all creation.

translation: To Him belongs what is in the heavens and what is on the earth and what is between them and what is under the soil
commentary:
لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا وَمَا تَحْتَ ٱلثَّرَىٰ

To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth and whatever is between them and whatever is under the soil.

Allah ﷻ now describes the scope of His dominion. He alone owns whatever exists in the skies, whatever exists on earth, whatever lies between the two, and—here comes something distinctive—whatever lies beneath الثرى, the wet layers of soil deep underground.

This final addition is unusual. Typically, when Allah ﷻ speaks of His ownership, He mentions the heavens, the earth, and what's between them, and the conversation ends. But in Surah Ta-Ha, He adds: "and whatever is under the wet soil."

The word ثرى  refers to the deepest, moistest layers of earth—the soil you reach when you dig far down. Why mention this specifically? Some scholars have noted that when discussing ownership, we naturally reference valuable things. No one boasts about owning a bottle cap. We talk about owning what has worth. Deep beneath the earth's surface lie tremendous treasures: oil, natural gas, coal, copper, and countless other resources. More than any previous age, our modern era has discovered the immense value of what lies buried in these depths.

But there's another reason for this mention, directly connected to this surah's narrative. We're going to learn about Fir'awn, and Fir'awn's final destination will be down under—buried deep beneath when Allah ﷻ parts the sea. The Israelites will walk on ground no human has walked before, and when the waters return, Fir'awn will be buried there. His final graveyard exists in those depths. Allah ﷻ is establishing: I own that space too.

translation: And if you speak aloud - then indeed, He knows the secret and what is [even] more hidden
commentary:
وَإِن تَجْهَرْ بِٱلْقَوْلِ فَإِنَّهُۥ يَعْلَمُ ٱلسِّرَّ وَأَخْفَى

And if you speak loudly—indeed, He knows the secret and what is even more hidden.

The word تجهر  means to call out loud—so loud that everyone around you can hear. There are various words for calling or speaking in Arabic (دعاء  , نداء  ), but جهر specifically means speaking loudly enough that others nearby can hear your words.

To illustrate: imagine you're in prayer or at Jumu'ah, and the person next to you has the habit of making extremely loud supplications. "O Allah, please help me pass my exam!" he calls out, and you and four other people can hear every word. You can't concentrate on your own prayer because his voice keeps breaking your focus. That's jahr.

Allah ﷻ is teaching us: even if you call upon Him loudly, you should know that He already knows السر (as-sirr) – the secret. Even if you kept your request completely private, hidden between you and Allah ﷻ, He already knew it before you voiced it.

But then Allah ﷻ adds something profound: وأخفى – "and what is even more hidden." What could be more hidden than a secret?

A secret is at least known by one person—the one keeping it. But there exist things even more hidden than secrets: things not yet discovered, not yet known even to ourselves. Allah ﷻ knows those too.

Consider this: sometimes you wake from a strange dream and wonder, "Where did that come from? That was weird." There are unconscious parts of your personality, suppressed memories from childhood, aspects of yourself you don't even recognize. A psychiatrist might use hypnosis to draw out some of these hidden elements, but whatever they can access and whatever they cannot, Allah ﷻ knows it all. He knows the parts of you that you yourself don't know.

These two concepts—the secret (السر) and what's even more hidden (أخفى)—will have specific applications to the story of Musa (AS) as this surah unfolds. Remember these terms, for we will return to them.

translation: Allah - there is no deity except Him. To Him belong the best names
commentary:
ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ

Allah—there is no deity except Him. To Him belong the Most Beautiful Names.

This declaration of tawheed brings together the opening passage. Allah ﷻ alone deserves worship and obedience in any way, shape, or form. And He alone possesses الأسماء الحسنى – the Most Beautiful Names.

Before revelation, the most beautiful of Allah's names were hidden from humanity—they were a secret. For people who didn't know Allah ﷻ at all, these names were even more hidden than a secret. He is the One who revealed them and made them known.

Each of Allah's names represents a different attribute and offers its own particular benefit. When you call upon Ar-Rahman, you're invoking His mercy. When you call upon Al-Khaliq (the Creator), you're acknowledging His creative power. When you call upon Al-Hakim (the Wise), you're recognizing His perfect wisdom. But running through all these names is one common thread: trust.

Trust is the foundation of our relationship with Allah ﷻ, regardless of which name we invoke. When we call Him the Wise, we trust His wisdom. When we call Him the Creator, we trust how He created us—we trust why He made us the height we are, with the intellect we have, speaking the language we speak, born into the family and ethnicity we were born into. When we call Him the Merciful, we trust that His mercy will come when needed and in full measure. When we call Him the All-Knowing, we trust what He knows, what He chose to let us know, what He chose to keep hidden from us, and we trust the circumstances that result from His knowledge.

Every one of His names provides multiple benefits, but they all build trust.

This is especially relevant given how this passage began. Allah ﷻ told the Prophet ﷺ: I didn't give you the Quran to put you in difficulty. You should have trust. And that trust will be built and strengthened by continually calling upon Allah ﷻ by His Most Beautiful Names.

Keep calling upon Allah ﷻ through His Most Beautiful Names, and watch how trust grows in your heart.

translation: And has the story of Moses reached you
commentary:
وَهَلْ أَتَىٰكَ حَدِيثُ مُوسَىٰٓ

And now the story begins. Allah ﷻ asks His Messenger ﷺ, "Has the news of Musa come to you?" The word حَدِيثُ refers to new information, something immediate that captures attention. In Arabic, we use هَادَثَة for an accident or something that just occurred. The word conveys fresh, attention-grabbing news.

What makes this phrasing remarkable is that the Prophet ﷺ had already heard about Musa (AS) before. Yet Allah ﷻ frames this as though it's the first time, using "Has the news come to you?" This teaches us something profound about receiving revelation. At this moment when the Prophet ﷺ is being instructed to place his trust in Allah ﷻ, he should approach this narration with the freshness and attentiveness of someone hearing it for the very first time.

translation: When he saw a fire and said to his family, "Stay here; indeed, I have perceived a fire; perhaps I can bring you a torch or find at the fire some guidance
commentary:
إِذْ رَءَا نَارًا فَقَالَ لِأَهْلِهِ ٱمْكُثُوٓا۟ إِنِّىٓ ءَانَسْتُ نَارًا لَّعَلِّىٓ ءَاتِيكُم مِّنْهَا بِقَبَسٍ أَوْ أَجِدُ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ هُدًى

The ayah opens with "when he saw a fire," immediately placing us in the midst of a journey. We know only what the believer in Makkah listening to this surah would know: Musa (AS) is traveling, he sees a fire, and he speaks to his family. An intelligent listener can piece together the context. If he saw a fire while traveling and could speak to his family, this must be nighttime—fires wouldn't be visible from far away during the day. He's traveling with his family in the darkness of night.

He tells them, ٱمْكُثُوٓا۟ - "stay here and wait." The word امْكُثْ means to remain in place, waiting in anticipation for someone's return. He instructs them to be patient until he gets back.

The ayah continues with a different verb for seeing: آنَسْتُ  - "I have observed." This word comes from أُنْس, which relates to affiliation and keen observation. آنَسَ implies being very observant of hints and drawing careful conclusions. It's nighttime, there's a mountain, and he catches just a flicker—not the fire itself, but perhaps the orange glow in the sky above it. The light appears, disappears, appears again briefly, then vanishes. By the time he tells his family, they can't see anything. But he's certain: "I know I saw something."

This is why the Quran uses آنَسْتُ. He's absolutely sure, even though others cannot confirm what he saw. Perhaps Allah ﷻ made this fire visible only to him, because no one else was supposed to go to this fire. This was a divinely scheduled encounter meant for Musa (AS) alone.

He tells his family his plan: "Perhaps I can bring you a قَبَس" - a borrowed flame. A qabas isn't an original fire but flame taken from another source, like lighting a torch from an existing fire to carry with you. "Or," he continues, "I might find at the fire some guidance." If there's a fire, there must be people camping, and if they're camping there, they must know where they are. Since Musa (AS) and his family are lost, he might get directions.

This moment teaches us something significant. If you're lost, there's no harm in seeking guidance. Musa (AS) tells his family to wait while he goes to get directions. There's no false pride here, no refusal to ask for help.

translation: And when he came to it, he was called, "O Moses
commentary:
فَلَمَّآ أَتَىٰهَا نُودِىَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ

فَلَمَّآ - "so eventually when..." The word أَتَىٰ indicates something that takes time and effort. Eventually, when he came upon it—imagine the climb up a dark mountain, with only the light above guiding him. When he finally reaches the top, نُودِىَ - a call is made. "O Musa!"

Consider his state of mind. He just told his family there's a fire up there, that he'll be back with flame or directions. It's dark. No one can see him climbing. No one knows he's there. Yet suddenly someone calls out his name—not "Hey, who's there?" Not "What are you doing here?" but specifically, deliberately: "Musa."

His mind must have been racing. Who is this? How do they know my name? Have I met this person before? And this isn't just any voice—it's an overwhelming, powerful voice. If you're already shocked while climbing a mountain, what might happen? You could lose your footing and fall. He's completely stunned at this point, his senses overtaken by what's happening.

translation: Indeed, I am your Lord, so remove your sandals. Indeed, you are in the sacred valley of Tuwa
commentary:
إِنِّىٓ أَنَا۠ رَبُّكَ فَٱخْلَعْ نَعْلَيْكَ ۖ إِنَّكَ بِٱلْوَادِ ٱلْمُقَدَّسِ طُوًى

"Indeed, I am your Master" - إِنِّىٓ أَنَا۠ رَبُّكَ. The voice continues with authority and clarity. Then comes the first instruction: فَٱخْلَعْ نَعْلَيْكَ - "Take off your shoes."

This is a fascinating moment to reflect upon. The first command given to our Prophet ﷺ was إِقْرَأْ  - "Read." The first command given to Musa (AS) is "Take off your shoes." Why? Because he's in a sacred place, and this is an act of respect and reverence.

This teaches us something essential about education. There are two components to true learning: knowledge and manners. Our Prophet ﷺ was given knowledge first. Musa (AS) was given manners first—the understanding of respect, humility, and reverence in the presence of authority. Both are indispensable parts of education.

Today we often emphasize only one component: academics and knowledge. You can obtain the highest degrees while never learning basic respect and manners. But here, in one of the most significant moments in history, Allah ﷻ teaches that reverence matters. Taking off your shoes is a show of respect to the authority before you and to the sacred ground beneath your feet.

إِنَّكَ بِٱلْوَادِ ٱلْمُقَدَّسِ طُوًى - "You are in the sacred valley of Tuwa." Musa (AS) still doesn't fully comprehend who he's speaking with. He heard "your Master," but he's in shock. He removes his shoes immediately, perhaps wondering, "Is Allah ﷻ really speaking to me, or am I losing my mind?"

translation: And I have chosen you, so listen to what is revealed [to you]
commentary:
وَأَنَا ٱخْتَرْتُكَ فَٱسْتَمِعْ لِمَا يُوحَىٰٓ

Then Allah ﷻ confirms: "And I have chosen you." You didn't come to this fire on your own. You didn't come because you decided to. I brought you here. I made you come here.

The word ٱخْتَرْتُكَ  comes from خَيْر  - goodness. When Allah ﷻ says "I have chosen you," it literally means "I have seen good in you." I chose you based on the good I see in you.

Now consider Musa's internal state. He carries a secret. He ran away from his homeland. He shared that secret with the elderly man who took him in: he had killed someone by accident. But there's a deeper secret within him, something he may not even fully recognize himself—the tremendous good in his heart, his genuine remorse over what happened, his sincere desire to do what's right. Allah ﷻ sees that good in him, even when he cannot see it in himself.

فَٱسْتَمِعْ لِمَا يُوحَىٰٓ - "So listen carefully to what is being revealed." Of course he will listen carefully—how could he not? But Allah ﷻ uses the emphatic form فَٱسْتَمِعْ  rather than the simple اِسْمَعْ . This exerts even greater authority, commanding complete, focused attention to the revelation that's about to come.

translation: Indeed, I am Allah. There is no deity except Me, so worship Me and establish prayer for My remembrance
commentary:
إِنَّنِىٓ أَنَا ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنَا۠ فَٱعْبُدْنِى وَأَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ لِذِكْرِىٓ

"Indeed, I am Allah. There is no deity worthy of worship except Me."

This is the only place in the entire Quran where Allah ﷻ declares His authority in the first person with such power and directness: إِنَّنِىٓ أَنَا ٱللَّهُ. There's no other place in the Quran quite like this. And what an extraordinary way to make this declaration—on top of a mountain, to a man alone, in the darkness of night. This is one of the greatest occasions in world history: a human being climbed a mountain and spoke directly with Allah ﷻ.

The Egyptians and Pharaoh, when they eventually learned how this revelation occurred, tried to mock it. Pharaoh told his minister Haman to build him a tower so he could go talk to God too. He thought it was a joke. He even told him to build it on sand and light a fire on top, mocking the very fire through which Musa (AS) received guidance. That's why Allah ﷻ described him as استَكْبَرَ - full of arrogance. He and his armies were consumed with pride.

فَٱعْبُدْنِى - "So worship Me, enslave yourself to Me." Then comes the most remarkable phrase: وَأَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ لِذِكْرِىٓ - "And establish the prayer to remember Me."

Let's pause here. When you meet someone famous, it's hard to forget. You try to take a picture, post it, frame it, put it on your wall. Meeting important people is a big deal to all of us. If you work at a large company and the CEO walks over to your desk, you'll tell everyone about it for months. You never forget, and you never let anyone else forget either.

Now imagine meeting someone infinitely more important than any human being. Musa (AS) had a private, personal conversation with Allah ﷻ Himself. Do you think he would ever forget this? Do you think he would ever let anyone else forget? When he comes down from that mountain to his waiting family, what do you imagine he would tell them first?

Yet with all of this—after this unprecedented, unforgettable encounter—Allah ﷻ tells him: "Establish the prayer in order to remember Me." He just spoke directly to Allah ﷻ. How could he possibly forget now? He's experienced something no human before him has experienced in quite this way. And yet Allah ﷻ tells him: "This is great what I'm doing with you, but the real way to remember Me is to establish prayer."

What we learn here is immense. We're jealous of Musa's position. He got to speak to Allah ﷻ directly. But Allah ﷻ tells him: Do what these believers do. Do what you're commanding others to do. Establish prayer. That's the real way of remembering Me. That's the constant way. That's when I make Myself available to you all the time.

He's at that exalted place on the mountain, and he's given the instruction to establish prayer. This is something our Prophet ﷺ shares in common with Musa (AS). Our Messenger ﷺ reached the highest place a human being has ever been—the night journey and ascension—and what instruction was he given there for his ummah? Prayer. Before him, the highest place reached in communication with Allah ﷻ was this mountain, and even there, what was given? Prayer.

وَأَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ لِذِكْرِىٓ - Establish prayer to remember Me. The emphasis continues.

translation: Indeed, the Hour is coming - I almost conceal it - so that every soul may be recompensed according to that for which it strives
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ ءَاتِيَةٌ أَكَادُ أُخْفِيهَا لِتُجْزَىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا تَسْعَىٰ

"Indeed, the Hour is coming." Establish prayer to remember Me—especially when you know the Hour is coming. The word آتِيَةٌ  implies something imminent, just around the corner. Musa (AS) was told it's right there, approaching. If that's what he was told thousands of years ago, what should we think about the Day of Judgment now? This gives even greater urgency to the command of prayer.

أَكَادُ أُخْفِيهَا - "I am almost keeping it hidden." Notice Allah ﷻ doesn't say "I am hiding it completely." He says "I am almost hiding it." This means: I will let some signs be known. Some indications I will reveal. I'll give you things to pay attention to, so you can know the Day of Judgment is drawing close.

One of the greatest signs is the coming of our Prophet ﷺ himself. When the Companions (RA) were listening to this conversation—this exchange between Musa (AS) and Allah ﷻ from thousands of years before—they were being told: "I'm keeping it almost hidden, but some signs I'll make apparent." The greatest of those signs was sitting right next to them, reciting the Quran. What sense of urgency the Companions (RA) must have felt!

لِتُجْزَىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا تَسْعَىٰ - "So that every soul may be recompensed for what it strives toward." The word جَزَاء (jaza) means complete compensation. Every person will be given complete recompense for whatever efforts he or she made, whatever they strove toward.

What's the connection between effort and the Day of Judgment? If Allah ﷻ didn't keep it somewhat hidden—if He announced it would happen on January 5th, 2013 at 4:00 PM Eastern Time—what would people do? They'd say, "Until January 4th, we can do whatever we want. At 4:59 PM on the 5th, we'll make repentance just in time."

Allah ﷻ kept it hidden so that people would put in constant effort, never knowing when their time will come. This is why He doesn't tell us when we're going to die. He could—He has that knowledge, and He could stamp it on our foreheads if He wanted. But He doesn't. Why? Because then we'd think we have time to waste, or we'd become depressed thinking "I'm going to die anyway, what's the point?"

لِتُجْزَىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا تَسْعَىٰ - so that every person may be compensated for the efforts they make. Life is about effort, about striving, about not knowing when our time will end and therefore making every moment count.

translation: So do not let one avert you from it who does not believe in it and follows his desire, for you [then] would perish
commentary:
فَلَا يَصُدَّنَّكَ عَنْهَا مَن لَّا يُؤْمِنُ بِهَا وَٱتَّبَعَ هَوَىٰهُ فَتَرْدَىٰ

"So do not let anyone distract you from it." The pronoun "it" - عَنْهَا  - is feminine, and there are two feminine concepts that came just before: السَّاعَةَ (the Hour, the Day of Judgment) and الصَّلَوٰةَ (the prayer). Both are feminine in Arabic, which creates a beautiful dual meaning. Don't let anyone take you away from prayer, and don't let anyone take you away from consciousness of the Hereafter.

These two things are actually one and the same. Someone who gets taken away from prayer is someone who, for those moments, has stopped caring about Judgment Day. That's the only way you can stop caring about your prayer. Someone who truly remembers the Hereafter will absolutely remember their prayer. And someone who maintains their prayer is constantly reinforcing their iman in the Day of Judgment. They support each other, strengthen each other, remind you of each other.

مَن لَّا يُؤْمِنُ بِهَا - "the one who doesn't believe in it." Believe in what? Two things: the one who doesn't believe in the Hereafter, and the one who doesn't believe in the value of prayer. They'll say, "What's the big deal? I'm a good person. I don't have to pray to be good." You'll hear these arguments. Allah ﷻ says: Don't let those kinds of people distract you.

وَٱتَّبَعَ هَوَىٰهُ - "and he follows his هَوَىٰ." The word هَوَىٰ refers to empty, baseless desires—whatever wind comes into his head, he follows. If you ask such a person, "Why are you doing that?" the most intelligent answer they can give is "I don't know. I just feel like it." They have no rationale even for themselves. "Why are you going there?" "I just wanna." That's how empty and baseless their reasoning is.

فَتَرْدَىٰ - "and then you will fall." The word تَرْدَىٰ is used figuratively to mean failure, not reaching your goals. But literally, رَدَىٰ means to fall off a cliff. Where is Musa (AS) right now? On top of a mountain. So Allah ﷻ is telling him: "Don't follow this person, because you will fail"—and the word used literally means you'll fall off a cliff.

This same word appears elsewhere in the Quran. A person who used to have a terrible friend finally realized that companion was no good for him and abandoned his company. On Judgment Day, he sees his old friend burning in Hell far below and says, "Man, I almost hung out with you!" He says: إِن كِدتَّ لَتُرْدِينِ - "You almost made me fall off the cliff too."

The cliff represents guidance. When you're up high on guidance and you fall, where do you fall? Down to the earth. You become worldly, materialistic. That's the imagery here. وَٱتَّبَعَ هَوَىٰهُ فَتَرْدَىٰ - he follows his empty desires, and then you fall from the height of guidance into the depths of worldly concern.

translation: And what is that in your right hand, O Moses
commentary:
**وَمَا تِلْكَ بِيَمِينِكَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ**

Allah ﷻ has been doing all the talking up to this point. He commanded Musa (AS) to remove his shoes, introduced Himself, commanded him to establish prayer, warned about the Day of Judgment, and cautioned against allowing anyone to distract him from prayer and the remembrance of the afterlife. Then, out of nowhere, Allah ﷻ asks a seemingly simple question: "And what is that in your right hand, Musa?"

Notice the phrase "يَـٰمُوسَىٰ" (O Musa). The conversation already began with this address, and there's nobody else around. If Allah ﷻ says "What's in your right hand?" it's already understood that He's speaking to Musa (AS). He's the only one there, and he's got something in his right hand, so it can't be confused with anyone else. This repetition of the name serves as a term of endearment, a way of calming him down.

There's an interesting psychological effect in mentioning someone's name at the end of a statement rather than at the beginning. When a parent calls their child's name at the end—"Come here, let me show you something, I'm so proud of you, I love you, Walid"—the mystery is gone, and any potential threat is removed. It becomes an expression of love. But when the name comes at the beginning, there's uncertainty: is there trouble or affection ahead? By placing Musa's name at the end, Allah ﷻ removes any sense of threat and creates an atmosphere of warmth.

There's another fascinating detail in the Arabic. Everything in Arabic is assumed masculine until proven feminine. When something is unknown, you would typically use the masculine demonstrative "ذَٰلِكَ" (that). But Allah ﷻ uses "تِلْكَ" (that-feminine) even before the question is answered. He asks, "What is *that feminine thing* in your hand?" This is significant because "عَصَا" (staff) is a standard feminine word in Arabic. By framing the question this way, Allah ﷻ is subtly indicating: I already know what's in your hand. I'm asking because I want you to relax and talk a little. This might calm you down, because you're still in shock from speaking directly to God.

It's like light conversation, a gentle changing of the subject to ease the tension.

translation: He said, "It is my staff; I lean upon it, and I bring down leaves for my sheep and I have therein other uses
commentary:
**قَالَ هِىَ عَصَاىَ أَتَوَكَّؤُا۟ عَلَيْهَا وَأَهُشُّ بِهَا عَلَىٰ غَنَمِى وَلِىَ فِيهَا مَـَٔارِبُ أُخْرَىٰ**

Now Musa (AS) is given the opportunity to address Allah ﷻ directly. We address Allah ﷻ all the time in prayer, but this is different—Allah ﷻ asked him to speak, posed a question, and gave him the opportunity to answer.

When people get nervous, some become tongue-tied. They've been wanting to meet someone their whole life, but when the moment arrives, nothing comes out. Others realize this is their only chance and seize the opportunity. The question is simple: "What's in your right hand?" The answer could be just as simple: "هِىَ عَصَاىَ" (It is my staff). The question is answered completely with this phrase. But Musa (AS) realizes this is an extraordinary opportunity. He should find ways to continue this conversation, to turn a one-sentence answer into an essay.

So he continues: "أَتَوَكَّؤُا۟ عَلَيْهَا" (I lean upon it). Allah ﷻ asked what it is, and he answered "my staff." The answer was complete. But he's thinking, "What else can I say? What else can I think of related to this stick?"

"وَأَهُشُّ بِهَا عَلَىٰ غَنَمِى" (And I bring down leaves with it for my sheep). The word "أَهُشُّ" is beautifully expressive in Arabic. It comes from the root meaning "to shake," but specifically to shake something soft. It's used to describe shaking leaves from a tree—when you use a stick to make soft leaves fall, the sound itself is captured in the word. It's also used to describe someone very sensitive, easily moved emotionally, or someone who smiles and giggles easily. The word itself paints the image: gently shaking branches so the leaves fall for the sheep to eat.

Now he's listed two substantial things he does with his staff. But he wants to keep going: "وَلِىَ فِيهَا مَـَٔارِبُ أُخْرَىٰ" (And I have other uses for it). The word "مَـَٔارِبُ" comes from a root meaning critical needs—absolutely essential tasks that if left unfulfilled would cause difficulty. He wants to make it sound important, valuable. He mentions "other stuff" but doesn't list it because he can't think of anything else in the moment.

When you have a chance to talk to someone important, you want to make the conversation longer, not shorter. This is such a beautiful insight into human nature—Musa (AS) wants to continue conversing with Allah ﷻ, to prolong this sacred exchange. It's a simple question about what's in his hand, but he doesn't yet know that Allah ﷻ has a larger plan with that question. The first part of the plan was to calm him down, and it worked—he's so relaxed now that he's casually talking about his stick, almost forgetting the magnitude of what's happening.

translation: [Allah] said, "Throw it down, O Moses
commentary:
**قَالَ أَلْقِهَا يَـٰمُوسَىٰ**

Very casually, Allah ﷻ says to him: "O Musa, throw it down." There are two words for throwing in Arabic: one means to hurl far away, and the other—"أَلْقِ"—means simply to drop down, to let fall. Allah ﷻ tells him to drop it, which means it won't fall far—it's going to be right there, close to him.

Again, notice "يَـٰمُوسَىٰ" (O Musa) is used, continuing to comfort him. If Allah ﷻ simply said "throw it down," it would already be understood as addressing Musa (AS). But the repeated use of his name is a means of maintaining that atmosphere of reassurance and closeness.

translation: So he threw it down, and thereupon it was a snake, moving swiftly
commentary:
**فَأَلْقَىٰهَا فَإِذَا هِىَ حَيَّةٌ تَسْعَىٰ**

He threw it down. Perhaps he thought it was another matter of etiquette, like removing the shoes. But then, "فَإِذَا" (and then, immediately, all of a sudden, unexpectedly) it turned into a snake. The word "فَإِذَا" indicates suddenness, something unexpected happening right away.

"حَيَّةٌ" (a snake) specifically refers to a snake whose fangs are visible. The word comes from roots meaning "life"—snakes in the desert were called this because they're a sign of life in the barren landscape, with their wavy movement resembling the waves of sand. But ironically, they also take life with one venomous bite. The word "تَسْعَىٰ" describes its movement, its slithering with fangs exposed.

And where is this snake? Right next to him. He's on top of a mountain, and remember—he doesn't have his shoes on. He's not protected. Can you imagine the proximity, the shock? First there was the initial shock of speaking to Allah ﷻ, but he had calmed down. Now, all of a sudden, terror.

The Quran gives us one line, and there's so much in between that we must fill in with our imagination. What happened in those moments? He must have been terrified. He must have jumped back, run to the other side of the fire. This is what Allah ﷻ does in storytelling—He tells you one detail, leaves space for you to imagine the human reality of it, then tells you the next detail.

translation: [Allah] said, "Seize it and fear not; We will return it to its former condition
commentary:
**قَالَ خُذْهَا وَلَا تَخَفْ ۖ سَنُعِيدُهَا سِيرَتَهَا ٱلْأُولَىٰ**

Allah ﷻ commands: "خُذْهَا" (Grab it). It's terrifying enough to be on top of a mountain with a fanged snake. But Allah ﷻ doesn't just say "don't be afraid, then grab it." He says: Grab it first. "وَلَا تَخَفْ" (And don't be afraid) comes second.

You would think the logical sequence would be: calm down, don't be afraid, *then* grab it. But Allah ﷻ reverses it: Grab it. And don't be afraid. Fear or no fear, My command comes first. What will help you execute the command will come later. Now it's time to get serious. Enough relaxation, enough casual conversation about the stick. Now: obey, immediately.

This is Musa (AS) being trained to deal with the real monster he'll have to face: Fir'awn. To face him, Musa (AS) will need to obey Allah ﷻ without hesitation, without thinking about consequences. He has to trust Allah ﷻ absolutely. Because grabbing a snake—if you're not trained—is essentially a death sentence. The fangs are out. Snakes strike with incredible speed. Even putting your hand near one is dangerous. But Allah ﷻ is training him to trust absolutely, no matter what the command is.

"سَنُعِيدُهَا سِيرَتَهَا ٱلْأُولَىٰ" (We will return it to its original form, its original characteristics). The word "سِيرَة" refers to all the features and characteristics something has—the complete nature of a thing. Allah ﷻ promises: We will bring it back to what it was before. You don't have to worry.

Notice there's no recorded conversation in between. No "O Allah, can we think about this?" No "Do You have gloves?" No negotiation. Which means he did it. He grabbed the snake. And it became a stick again. He must have stood there, stunned: it's a stick again.

translation: And draw in your hand to your side; it will come out white without disease - another sign
commentary:
**وَٱضْمُمْ يَدَكَ إِلَىٰ جَنَاحِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضَآءَ مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوٓءٍ ءَايَةً أُخْرَىٰ**

Now Allah ﷻ tells him: "وَٱضْمُمْ يَدَكَ إِلَىٰ جَنَاحِكَ" (And draw your hand into your armpit). Not just place it there, but press it in, squeeze it into your armpit. "تَخْرُجْ بَيْضَآءَ" (It will come out white)—"مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوٓءٍ" (without any fault or blemish whatsoever). Not diseased white, not skin-condition white. Pure, radiant white. You can't even see skin pores—just fluorescent, brilliant white light emanating from it.

Now he has a staff that transforms into a snake in one hand, and his other hand has become like a light bulb, radiating white light. "ءَايَةً أُخْرَىٰ" (Another miraculous sign). Now he's absolutely certain he's dealing with Allah ﷻ. There's no doubt left.

translation: That We may show you [some] of Our greater signs
commentary:
**لِنُرِيَكَ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا ٱلْكُبْرَى**

"لِنُرِيَكَ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا ٱلْكُبْرَى" (So that We may show you some of Our greatest signs). These aren't small signs—these are among the greatest miracles. But first they had to be shown to him so he would learn to trust Allah ﷻ completely. Now he knows how to use them.

translation: Go to Pharaoh. Indeed, he has transgressed
commentary:
**ٱذْهَبْ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُۥ طَغَىٰ**

And then comes the transition: "ٱذْهَبْ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ" (Go to Fir'awn). This is a striking shift. Musa (AS) has just had probably the most extraordinary experience of his life—undoubtedly the most amazing experience in human existence up to that point. A direct conversation with Allah ﷻ, witnessing His greatest signs, experiencing His gentleness and reassurance.

And now he's being asked to transition from the most beautiful conversation to the ugliest possible conversation. "You're done talking to God. Now go talk to Fir'awn."

The prophets have to do two things. They train themselves and build their trust through the most beautiful conversation with Allah ﷻ. Then, when they're ready, they have to go engage in the ugliest conversations with people. Because people have no respect, they don't hold back, they're not courteous. They lie, they accuse, they mock, they insult. The prophets are able to deal with all of that from the strength they gain by conversing with Allah ﷻ.

So there are two conversations happening in a prophet's life: conversing with Allah ﷻ, and conversing with people. And in this case, the worst of all people—Fir'awn. To have that incredibly difficult conversation, the first conversation prepared him for the next.

This is true of our Prophet ﷺ as well. He said: "The coolness of my eyes was placed in prayer." Prayer is where the Prophet ﷺ talks to Allah ﷻ, where he's calm and at peace. But when the prayer is over, he has to walk into the storm—dealing with all the people and all the horrible things they say. The prayer prepares him. That's what Surah Al-Muzzammil teaches us: stand in prayer all night, half the night, a third of the night. Why? "إِنَّا سَنُلْقِي عَلَيْكَ قَوْلًا ثَقِيلًا" (We're going to place upon you a heavy word). The heavy word comes in the next surah: "يَا أَيُّهَا الْمُدَّثِّرُ قُمْ فَأَنذِرْ" (O you wrapped in garments, arise and warn). Talk to Allah ﷻ in one surah, then go warn the people in the next.

When the Prophet's ﷺ mission was finally accomplished—when victory came and people were entering Islam in crowds—what does the final surah say? "فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَاسْتَغْفِرْهُ" (Then glorify the praises of your Lord and ask His forgiveness). Why? Because that's what you love doing. That's what you've always wanted. If it were up to the prophets, they would only do remembrance of Allah ﷻ. But Allah ﷻ tasks them with going out to deal with people, to warn and engage.

This balance is now being established for Musa (AS). He's being sent to Fir'awn. "إِنَّهُۥ طَغَىٰ" (Indeed, he has transgressed). No doubt—he is the one who has rebelled beyond all bounds.

translation: [Moses] said, "My Lord, expand for me my breast [with assurance]
commentary:
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱشْرَحْ لِى صَدْرِى

Musa (AS) begins his supplication with a profound request: "My Lord, expand my chest for me." This opening plea reveals the depth of his understanding about what lies ahead. The word شَرْح  carries multiple layers of meaning that illuminate the nature of this request.

On the most accessible level, Musa (AS) is asking for confidence. He recognizes that standing before Fir'awn will require unwavering inner strength. But the request goes deeper than mere courage. When Allah ﷻ expands someone's chest for a matter, it means they become completely focused and in tune with their task. Their mind is sharp, their attention is fully present, and they are, as we might say, "in the zone." There is no distraction, no mental fog, just clarity of purpose.

This expansion also encompasses complete relaxation in the face of difficulty. Musa (AS) is asking to remain calm, neither nervous nor fearful, neither lazy nor overwhelmed. He wants to maintain his composure regardless of what Fir'awn might say or do.

This request takes on special significance when we consider Musa's temperament. He is known for his passionate nature, his quick anger when faced with injustice. This is the same Musa (AS) who, in a future moment of frustration, will grab his brother Harun (AS) by the beard and then by the head. His brother will have to plead twice, "Don't grab my beard, don't grab my head," because letting go of one meant Musa (AS) immediately grabbed the other. He has a temper, and he knows it.

Before Fir'awn, who will say the most obnoxious and blasphemous things, Musa (AS) knows he must remain calm. If he allows anger to overtake him, he won't be able to fulfill his mission properly. This is why his very first request is for an expanded chest, for contentment and composure that will carry him through the confrontation ahead.

It's worth noting that our Prophet Muhammad ﷺ received this gift without even asking. Allah ﷻ says to him, أَلَمْ نَشْرَحْ لَكَ صَدْرَكَ - "Have We not expanded your chest for you?" The Prophet ﷺ was granted this blessing as a divine favor, while Musa (AS) had to request it, recognizing his own need for this strength.

translation: And ease for me my task
commentary:
وَيَسِّرْ لِىٓ أَمْرِى

"And make my task easy for me." The mission Musa (AS) has been given is, by its very nature, extraordinarily difficult. To stand before the most powerful tyrant of the age and call him to accountability, to demand the freedom of an enslaved nation, to speak truth to absolute power—this is no small assignment. Musa (AS) recognizes that only Allah ﷻ can make such an overwhelming task manageable.

This is a beautiful acknowledgment of human limitation and divine capability. We can be given the most daunting responsibilities, but with Allah's facilitation, what seems impossible becomes achievable. The word يَسِّرْ  asks for ease, for the removal of obstacles, for divine smoothing of a path that would otherwise be too treacherous to walk.

translation: And untie the knot from my tongue
commentary:
وَٱحْلُلْ عُقْدَةً مِّن لِّسَانِى

"And untie the knot from my tongue." Here Musa (AS) identifies a specific challenge he knows he will face. The Arabic expression عُقْدَة  literally means "knot," and Musa (AS) describes it as being مِّن لِّسَانِى —from within his tongue, afflicting his tongue.

This carries several meanings. First, there is the literal speech impediment—a stutter that affects Musa (AS) when he speaks. Historical accounts and other passages in the Quran reference this difficulty with speech. A stutter is challenging enough under normal circumstances, but when someone with a stutter becomes nervous or angry, the impediment intensifies. They struggle to get even two syllables out clearly.

But the "knot in the tongue" means something more than just a physical stutter. It refers to the jumbling of speech that can happen under pressure. Imagine preparing a logical argument with points A, B, C, D, and E that must be presented in order for them to make sense. Then, in the moment of nervousness, you start with C, forget about A, circle back to B, skip to F by mistake, then remember D should have come first—and suddenly your entire presentation is incoherent. Your audience is left confused, unable to follow your reasoning. This is the tongue being "tied up," the knot that prevents clear expression.

Musa (AS) knows he will be giving a very public speech before Fir'awn. This won't be a private conversation; it will happen before Fir'awn's court, with generals and officials present. In such a setting, if nervousness overtakes him, if the stutter worsens, if his points become jumbled, the entire message could be lost. He is asking Allah ﷻ for clarity—for his speech to flow logically, for his argument to be coherent and compelling.

This is a dua we find answered later in the Quran. While we see the request here in Surah Ta-Ha, the fulfillment of this particular prayer is beautifully displayed in Surah Ash-Shu'ara, where we witness how effectively Musa (AS) speaks before Fir'awn, his tongue no longer hindered but flowing with divine eloquence.

translation: That they may understand my speech
commentary:
يَفْقَهُوا۟ قَوْلِى

"So that they may understand my speech." This short but powerful statement reveals a crucial principle of communication, especially when conveying the message of Allah ﷻ. The purpose of speaking is not merely to express your thoughts, but to express them in a way that others can genuinely understand.

The word يَفْقَهُوا  comes from فِقْه , which means deep understanding and comprehension. Musa (AS) isn't asking simply that people hear his words or get a vague sense of what he's talking about. He wants them to deeply grasp his message, to walk away with clear understanding, not just bits and pieces they struggle to put together later.

This reflects a profound awareness of audience. You may have extensive knowledge, a certain level of expertise, years of study behind you—but if you speak at your own level without considering who is listening, your message will fail. The great principle here is that a speaker must meet people at the level of their understanding, not remain at the level of their own comfort.

The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ taught this explicitly: "Address people at the level of their intellect." This is the difference between a scholar and a teacher. A scholar, when their mouth opens, can be like a burst pipe—knowledge flowing out in such volume and complexity that the audience is overwhelmed. They walk away thinking, "That was a lot of knowledge, but what exactly did they say?" A teacher, on the other hand, may possess the same depth of knowledge but takes people step by step, ensuring comprehension before moving forward.

Musa (AS) demonstrates the mindset of a true teacher here. He is more concerned with what his audience understands than with what he says. He wants the right words to emerge, words calibrated to the hearts and minds of those listening.

It's worth reflecting that as the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ recites these words to the Sahaba (RA), there is a parallel reality. Musa (AS) is asking for the right words and the ability to be understood. But our Prophet ﷺ doesn't have to construct his own speech—Allah ﷻ is giving him the perfect words through revelation. The Quran itself is the answer to this dua that Musa (AS) made. The Prophet ﷺ and his companions are constantly comparing the situation of Musa (AS) with their own, drawing lessons and finding comfort in these parallels.

translation: And appoint for me a minister from my family
commentary:
وَٱجْعَل لِّى وَزِيرًا مِّنْ أَهْلِى

"And appoint for me a minister from my family." The word وَزِير  comes from وِزْر , which means burden—specifically, a huge burden or weight of responsibility. A wazir, then, is someone who carries a tremendous load, someone who takes on responsibility and thereby lightens your burden. This is the origin of the English word "vizier" or "minister."

Musa (AS) recognizes he cannot carry this mission alone. The task ahead is too immense, too demanding. He needs someone by his side, someone who shares the weight, someone who can support him when he falters. And he doesn't ask for just anyone—he asks for someone مِّنْ أَهْلِى , from his own family. He wants someone he can trust completely, someone whose loyalty is beyond question, someone who knows him intimately enough to provide the right support at the right moment.

translation: Aaron, my brother
commentary:
هَـٰرُونَ أَخِى

"Harun, my brother." Notice that Allah ﷻ already knows who Musa's brother is, yet Musa (AS) says أَخِى —"my brother." This isn't for Allah's information; it's an expression of love and affection. By saying "my brother," Musa (AS) reveals the depth of his bond with Harun (AS). He is happy, grateful even, that he has a brother. He loves his brother, and that love comes through in how he mentions him.

This ayah stands alone, separate from what comes before and after. When an ayah stands by itself in the Quran, it is an invitation to reflect. Pause here, Allah ﷻ seems to say. Consider the love between these two brothers. Appreciate the blessing of brotherhood, of family bonds, of having someone who stands with you.

translation: Increase through him my strength
commentary:
ٱشْدُدْ بِهِۦٓ أَزْرِى

"Strengthen my back through him." The word أَزْر  refers to the back, and by extension, one's support and strength. Musa (AS) is asking that Harun (AS) be the one who reinforces him when he begins to shake, who steadies him when he falters, who gives him strength when his own is failing.

Picture a boxer in the ring with a coach at their side. The coach doesn't fight for them, but in moments of exhaustion or doubt, they lean in: "Come on, you've got this. Stay strong. Keep going." That's the role Musa (AS) envisions for Harun (AS). When he stands before Fir'awn and hears him claim divinity, when Musa's fist clenches in anger, he needs Harun (AS) to grab his hand and whisper, "Relax, brother. It's okay. Let it go." He needs that calming presence, that moral support, that coaching voice.

translation: And let him share my task
commentary:
وَأَشْرِكْهُ فِىٓ أَمْرِى

"And make him a partner in my task." The word أَشْرِكْ  here means to make someone a full partner, a co-participant. Musa (AS) isn't asking for an assistant or a helper who follows orders. He wants Harun (AS) to be truly involved, to share in the responsibility, to be with him in every aspect of this mission.

This shows remarkable humility. Allah ﷻ has chosen Musa (AS) specifically for this task, spoken to him directly, given him the signs. Yet Musa (AS) says, "I need my brother with me." He recognizes that some missions are too great for one person, no matter how capable, no matter how divinely supported. The presence of Harun (AS) isn't a sign of weakness; it's wisdom.

The repeated mention of Harun (AS) in these ayahs signals his importance. One entire ayah is dedicated just to his name: "Harun, my brother." This emphasis is unique to Surah Ta-Ha. In other surahs where the story of Musa (AS) appears, most of the verbs and actions are in the singular, referring to Musa (AS) alone. But in this surah, many of the verbs shift to the dual form, indicating two people acting together. When the Quran uses the dual, it's highlighting Harun (AS)—showing that this mission belongs to both brothers, that Harun's role is integral, not incidental.

translation: That we may exalt You much
commentary:
كَىْ نُسَبِّحَكَ كَثِيرًا

"So that we may glorify You much." Here we see the first use of "we"—both brothers together. The word نُسَبِّح  means to declare Allah's perfection, to glorify Him, to engage in tasbih. The addition of كَثِيرًا —"much" or "abundantly"—emphasizes that this will be constant, frequent, and plentiful.

Now, you might wonder: Musa (AS) is being told to go speak to Fir'awn. What does tasbih have to do with confronting a tyrant? This is where we see profound wisdom. Musa (AS) understands that before they can deal with Fir'awn, before they can face external evil, they must first strengthen their connection with Allah ﷻ. The tasbih is their source of strength. The remembrance of Allah ﷻ is their foundation.

He's saying, "My brother and I will remind each other. When it's time for worship, when one of us is tired or distracted, the other will encourage him. We will keep each other spiritually strong through constant glorification of You." This is the inner work that enables the outer mission. Without a firm spiritual foundation, without constant dhikr and tasbih, they cannot hope to succeed in such a daunting task.

translation: And remember You much
commentary:
وَنَذْكُرَكَ كَثِيرًا

"And remember You much." After mentioning tasbih (glorification), Musa (AS) mentions ذِكْر —remembrance. While tasbih is a form of dhikr, the broader term encompasses all forms of remembering Allah ﷻ: through prayer, through reflection, through constant awareness of His presence.

Again, we see كَثِيرًا —much, abundantly. This isn't occasional remembrance when convenient. This is continuous, pervasive awareness of Allah ﷻ throughout their day. "Sometimes I will wake my brother for prayer, sometimes he will wake me. We will remind each other constantly. Our hearts will stay connected to You through abundant remembrance."

This is the secret to their success: spiritual partnership. Two brothers, each keeping the other's faith strong, each pulling the other toward worship when human weakness threatens, each reinforcing the other's connection to Allah ﷻ.

translation: Indeed, You are of us ever Seeing
commentary:
إِنَّكَ كُنتَ بِنَا بَصِيرًا

"Indeed, You have always been, of us, Seeing." The word بَصِير  means All-Seeing, but when Allah ﷻ is described as بَصِير بِنَا —Seeing of us—it implies not just awareness but care, watchfulness, and protection.

Musa (AS) is acknowledging something profound here. "You have been watching over both of us all this time, not just me." This is significant because we know how Musa (AS) survived. He was placed in a basket in the river, picked up by Fir'awn's household, and raised in the palace. His entire youth was spent in protection, hidden in plain sight. Allah's Hand was clearly guiding his survival.

But what about Harun (AS)? He was a regular Israelite, living among the enslaved people. He had no palace protection, no special status. During the years when Fir'awn was ordering the execution of Israelite baby boys every other year, Harun (AS) survived. He grew up in those conditions of oppression and danger. That he is alive, healthy, and capable of joining this mission is itself a miracle.

Musa (AS) recognizes this. "You protected both of us, through different paths, for this moment. You have always been seeing us, watching over us, preparing us." There is deep gratitude in this acknowledgment, and also trust. The same Allah ﷻ who protected them through childhood and youth will continue to watch over them in the mission ahead.

translation: [Allah] said, "You have been granted your request, O Moses
commentary:
قَالَ قَدْ أُوتِيتَ سُؤْلَكَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ

Allah ﷻ responds: "He said: You have been granted your request, O Musa." But the Arabic carries additional layers. The phrase قَدْ أُوتِيتَ  suggests something already decided, already done before the asking was even complete. It's as though Allah ﷻ is saying, "Before you finished your dua, I had already decided to grant it all."

The word سُؤْل  is particularly interesting. It comes from the root meaning "that which is asked," but the specific form used here—su'l rather than mas'ul—expands the meaning. Su'l means not only what has been asked, but also what is needed, even if not explicitly requested.

This distinction is beautiful. Musa (AS) asked for specific things: an expanded chest, ease in his task, clarity of speech, his brother by his side. But there were things he didn't ask for that Allah ﷻ knew he needed: the miraculous staff that turns into a snake, the hand that glows white, the nine signs that will be given to him. All of these are included in "you have been granted your su'l"—not just what you asked for, but everything you need for this mission, whether you thought to ask for it or not.

This is the generosity of Allah ﷻ. He doesn't merely respond to our explicit requests; He knows what we truly need and provides it in full measure.

translation: And We had already conferred favor upon you another time
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ مَنَنَّا عَلَيْكَ مَرَّةً أُخْرَىٰ

"And We had certainly conferred favor upon you another time." The word مَنَّ  is rich with meaning. As a noun, مَنّ means weight, specifically a large measure of weight. As a verb in this context, it means to grant a favor, but a favor that is substantial, weighty, significant. Combining these meanings, مَنَّ conveys the granting of a huge, heavy favor—something of tremendous value.

There's another nuance to the word مَنّ. It can also mean to remind someone of a favor you've done for them. Usually, when humans remind others of favors, it's considered poor character. But when Allah ﷻ does it, it serves a different purpose—to help us recognize His blessings, to inspire gratitude, to strengthen our certainty in His care.

Allah ﷻ is saying to Musa (AS), "I've just granted you everything you asked for and more. But let me remind you—this isn't the first time I've shown you extraordinary favor." The phrase مَرَّةً أُخْرَىٰ —"another time"—points back to a previous moment of divine intervention, one so significant that Musa (AS) owes his very life to it.

This is both comforting and strengthening. If Allah ﷻ saved you once in miraculous fashion, He can certainly protect and guide you now. The past favor is evidence of future care.

translation: When We inspired to your mother what We inspired
commentary:
إِذْ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أُمِّكَ مَا يُوحَىٰ

"When We inspired your mother with what We inspired." The phrase إِذْ  marks a specific time in the past, taking us back to that earlier moment of divine favor. But notice the phrasing: أَوْحَيْنَا —"We revealed or inspired"—followed by مَا يُوحَىٰ—"what is revealed/inspired."

This construction is deliberately mysterious. It's like saying, "We told her what We told her," or "We revealed what We revealed." The content is left somewhat veiled, creating an air of sacred privacy. Why?

The revelation given to Musa's mother included instructions that are detailed in other surahs—most notably, that she should nurse her baby, then place him in a basket in the river. But here, in this conversation with Musa (AS) himself, Allah ﷻ doesn't explicitly mention the nursing. This is out of respect for his mother, maintaining her حَيَاء —her modesty and dignity.

The conversation is happening with Musa (AS), and there's a certain reserve, a certain honor maintained by not spelling out every intimate detail of his mother's experience. Later, in Surah Al-Qasas, when the story is being told to us as an audience, those details will be shared. But here, with Musa (AS) as the primary listener, the phrasing remains respectfully general.

This subtlety shows us something profound about the Quran's communication style. Allah ﷻ doesn't just convey information; He conveys it with wisdom, sensitivity, and respect for the dignity of those involved. Even in revealing divine history, the manner of revelation considers the honor of Musa's mother (may Allah ﷻ be pleased with her) and the sacred bond between mother and son.

The favor being recalled here is monumental: through divine inspiration to his mother, Musa's life was preserved when all Israelite boys were being killed. That protection, that guidance, that miraculous survival—it was all from Allah ﷻ. And if He could do that then, He will certainly continue His favor now.

translation: [Saying], 'Cast him into the chest and cast it into the river, and the river will throw it onto the bank; there will take him an enemy to Me and an enemy to him.' And I bestowed upon you love from Me that you would be brought up under My eye
commentary:
أَنِ ٱقْذِفِيهِ فِى ٱلتَّابُوتِ فَٱقْذِفِيهِ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ فَلْيُلْقِهِ ٱلْيَمُّ بِٱلسَّاحِلِ يَأْخُذْهُ عَدُوٌّ لِّى وَعَدُوٌّ لَّهُۥ ۚ وَأَلْقَيْتُ عَلَيْكَ مَحَبَّةً مِّنِّى وَلِتُصْنَعَ عَلَىٰ عَيْنِىٓ

This ayah captures Allah's ﷻ instructions to the mother of Musa (AS), revealed to her directly in those moments of absolute terror when soldiers were searching for Hebrew babies. The command begins with اقْذِفِيهِ - throw him into the chest. But notice the choice of verb here: قذف  means to throw something far, to hurl it with force, not simply to place it gently. The instruction continues: فَٱقْذِفِيهِ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ - then throw him into the river. Again, that same powerful verb. The original plan might have been for her to simply place the basket near the water's edge, but circumstances changed. When she saw soldiers approaching, she realized they would spot a basket floating nearby. She had to throw it far out into the current.

The term يَمّ refers specifically to a wide, expansive river - not just any stream. The Nile qualified as a yamm, vast and intimidating. Allah ﷻ then describes what would happen next: فَلْيُلْقِهِ ٱلْيَمُّ بِٱلسَّاحِلِ - the river itself will cast him onto the shore. The river becomes an active participant in this divine plan, carrying the baby safely and depositing him at a specific location on the bank.

Then comes the most startling part of these instructions: يَأْخُذْهُ عَدُوٌّ لِّى وَعَدُوٌّ لَّهُۥ - an enemy of Mine and an enemy of his will take him. Imagine being that mother, receiving comfort that someone will find your child and care for him, only to hear that this someone will be Allah's ﷻ enemy and the child's enemy. The only person who could truly be described as an enemy to both Allah ﷻ and this innocent baby was Fir'awn himself - the very man who had ordered the slaughter of Hebrew boys. The baby would end up at the doorstep of the actual killer, in the house of the man who wanted him dead.

This brings us to the next divine favor: وَأَلْقَيْتُ عَلَيْكَ مَحَبَّةً مِّنِّى - and I cast upon you a special love from Me. Notice the abundance of "throwing" in this passage: throw him in the chest, throw him in the river, the river will throw him on the shore, Allah ﷻ will throw love upon him. But this throwing of love was unique and critical.

The Arabic language has two primary words for love: حُبّ  and مَحَبَّة . Hubb appears about eleven times in the Quran, but mahabbah appears only this once. The form mahabbah is what grammarians call اسم المصدر , a more intensive form indicating a super-charged, extraordinary love. What makes it even more special is that Allah ﷻ uses the first person singular - أَلْقَيْتُ, "I threw" - rather than the royal plural "We." This personalization intensifies the meaning. Then He ﷻ adds مِّنِّى - from Me - as if to say this love came exclusively from Him, a direct gift.

What did this special love mean practically? Anyone who opened that chest, anyone who looked at this child, would be overcome with affection. Some babies make you think "that's nice," but this baby made people think "I need to hold him right now." This wasn't just about being cute - this was a supernatural endearment placed upon him by Allah ﷻ. It was a lifesaver.

When a soldier picked up the basket, instead of thinking "here's one that got away," he looked at the baby and melted. He passed the child to someone else, who passed him to another, until eventually the basket reached Fir'awn himself - a man with no mercy, a murderer of infants. Yet when his wife Asiyah held up the baby to him, she said قُرَّةُ عَيْنٍ لِّي وَلَكَ - a coolness of the eyes for me and for you (28:9). She couldn't take her eyes off him, and even Fir'awn, this psychopathic tyrant, looked at the baby and calmed down. Even he softened, even he felt something. Had this mahabbah not been upon Musa (AS), Fir'awn would have simply ordered "kill him, be done with it." But he couldn't.

This special love became a shield around Musa (AS) throughout his life. Years later, when he returned to Egypt as a wanted man - someone with an outstanding murder charge - he wasn't arrested at the gates. The doors of the palace opened for him. The guards, who should have recognized him from wanted notices, instead let him pass. Even when he stood before Fir'awn and spoke words that were deeply offensive to the tyrant in his own throne room, Fir'awn still didn't execute him. The mahabbah remained.

But there's another dimension to this love. Musa (AS) had a special love for Allah ﷻ, and Allah ﷻ had a special love for him. This is why Musa (AS) got to have actual conversations with Allah ﷻ, why Allah ﷻ asked him intimate questions like "What is in your right hand?" before commanding him to throw down his staff. He ﷻ could have simply said "Musa, throw your staff," but instead He ﷻ initiated a beautiful dialogue. This is the fruit of divine love - closeness, conversation, connection.

The ayah concludes with وَلِتُصْنَعَ عَلَىٰ عَيْنِىٓ - and so that you would be raised under My watchful eye. The word تُصْنَعَ  comes from صناعة , which means to manufacture something, to build something piece by piece with careful precision. Allah ﷻ wasn't just protecting Musa (AS); He ﷻ was carefully crafting his personality, his skills, his character. 

Under Allah's ﷻ watchful eye, Musa (AS) was raised in the palace of Fir'awn, learning the inner workings of the system. He developed personal relationships with ministers and generals. He received the highest education available - Egyptology tells us that the palaces of the pharaohs were like the Harvard and Yale of that era, where kings from other nations would send their children to be educated. Musa (AS) understood how power operated because he was raised within it.

But his upbringing wasn't limited to the palace. He had two remarkable mothers - his biological mother and Asiyah, one of the greatest women in human history. He had a clever older sister and a younger brother. All of these relationships, all of these experiences were part of Allah's ﷻ careful manufacturing of Musa (AS), preparing him for the monumental mission ahead.

translation: [And We favored you] when your sister went and said, 'Shall I direct you to someone who will be responsible for him?' So We restored you to your mother that she might be content and not grieve. And you killed someone, but We saved you from retaliation and tried you with a [severe] trial. And you remained [some] years among the people of Madyan. Then you came [here] at the decreed time, O Moses
commentary:
إِذْ تَمْشِىٓ أُخْتُكَ فَتَقُولُ هَلْ أَدُلُّكُمْ عَلَىٰ مَن يَكْفُلُهُۥ ۖ فَرَجَعْنَـٰكَ إِلَىٰٓ أُمِّكَ كَىْ تَقَرَّ عَيْنُهَا وَلَا تَحْزَنَ ۚ وَقَتَلْتَ نَفْسًا فَنَجَّيْنَـٰكَ مِنَ ٱلْغَمِّ وَفَتَنَّـٰكَ فُتُونًا ۚ فَلَبِثْتَ سِنِينَ فِىٓ أَهْلِ مَدْيَنَ ثُمَّ جِئْتَ عَلَىٰ قَدَرٍ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ

The narration continues with remarkable conciseness: إِذْ تَمْشِىٓ أُخْتُكَ - when your sister walked. That's it. No additional details. Where was she walking? Was she following the basket along the riverbank? Was she at the palace gates? Was she inside somehow? Allah ﷻ leaves these questions unanswered, creating curiosity in the listener. This is the art of Quranic storytelling - sometimes details are withheld deliberately, making you wonder, making you want to piece together the full picture from other passages that will come later.

What we do learn is what she said: هَلْ أَدُلُّكُمْ عَلَىٰ مَن يَكْفُلُهُۥ - shall I direct you to someone who might take care of him? Notice her intelligence in this phrasing. She doesn't say "I'll tell you about the woman who will nurse him" or "there's this specific person I know." She keeps it vague - "someone who might be able to care for him." She acts as if she has no connection to this woman, no personal knowledge. She's protecting her mother's identity, being careful not to arouse suspicion. This cleverness would become a hallmark of the family of Musa (AS).

Then Allah ﷻ describes the outcome: فَرَجَعْنَـٰكَ إِلَىٰٓ أُمِّكَ - so We returned you to your mother. But look at how Allah ﷻ frames the reason: كَىْ تَقَرَّ عَيْنُهَا وَلَا تَحْزَنَ - so that her eyes would be cooled and she would not grieve. 

Wait. This entire conversation is Allah ﷻ recounting His favors to Musa (AS) on the mountain. Every previous statement has been about what Allah ﷻ did for him personally. But suddenly, the favor mentioned is about his mother's happiness - that her eyes would find coolness, that she wouldn't be sad. Why count that as a favor to Musa (AS)?

This teaches us something profound about the parent-child relationship. The joy of your mother is your joy. Your mother being happy is a favor Allah ﷻ has given to you, not just to her. Just as a mother feels happiness when her child is happy, the reverse should also be true - you should feel joy when your parents are happy. Allah ﷻ considers His gift of happiness to Musa's mother as a blessing He bestowed upon Musa (AS) himself. For young people especially, this is crucial to understand: your parents' contentment is part of your own blessing.

The ayah then races through the next major chapter of Musa's (AS) life: وَقَتَلْتَ نَفْسًا - and you killed a person. This refers to the incident where Musa (AS) struck a man who died as a result. فَنَجَّيْنَـٰكَ مِنَ ٱلْغَمِّ - and We saved you from the ghamm. 

The word غَمّ is often translated as grief or distress, but its deeper meaning relates to darkness. It comes from the imagery of dark clouds, of being in a place without light, without windows. Imagine being in a prison where you can't see outside, where you don't know when you'll be released, where everything around you is uncertain and suffocating. That's ghamm - it's sadness combined with darkness, anxiety, and hopelessness. Allah ﷻ rescued Musa (AS) from that overwhelming state.

وَفَتَنَّـٰكَ فُتُونًا - and We tested you with various trials. The plural form فُتُونًا indicates multiple tests, numerous challenges. But Allah ﷻ doesn't specify what these tests were. Just as Musa (AS) told Allah ﷻ "I have many critical needs," and Allah ﷻ responded "They're all fulfilled," here Allah ﷻ says "I tested you in many ways" without elaborating. This creates curiosity - what were those other trials? The answer comes in other surahs, in other parts of Musa's story.

فَلَبِثْتَ سِنِينَ فِىٓ أَهْلِ مَدْيَنَ - then you stayed for years among the people of Madyan. Between the lines, we understand: Musa (AS) killed someone, fled Egypt, and ended up in Madyan. How did he get there? Why Madyan specifically? These details are filled in elsewhere, but the Quran here gives us only the essential point - he spent years there, away from Egypt.

Then comes one of the most beautiful statements in the entire surah: ثُمَّ جِئْتَ عَلَىٰ قَدَرٍ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ - then you came right on qadar, O Musa. The word قَدَر  means divine decree, predestination, precise calculation and schedule. Allah ﷻ is saying: your arrival at this mountain, at this moment, was scheduled from the time you were a baby. Everything that happened was precisely calculated, exactly on time.

Think about what this means. If that baby hadn't been cute enough, he would have been killed - no meeting on the mountain. If his mother hadn't thrown him far enough into the river, he would have been discovered - no meeting. If he hadn't ended up in Fir'awn's house, he wouldn't have received that education - no meeting. If he hadn't killed that person, he never would have fled. If he never fled, he never would have reached Madyan. If he never reached Madyan, he never would have met the elderly man. If he never met that man, he never would have married one of his daughters. If he never married, he never would have worked there for eight or ten years. If he never worked there, he never would have eventually said to his father-in-law "I'm leaving." If he never left, he never would have gotten lost in the desert. If he never got lost, he never would have seen the fire. If he never saw the fire, he never would have told his family "wait here, I'll investigate."

Every single piece had to fall into place. Any one element missing, and this conversation on the mountain doesn't happen. If Musa (AS) had decided "ten years in Madyan is enough, I'm comfortable here, the economy is tough elsewhere, I'll just stay" - none of this happens. He had to leave at that specific time. He had to get lost at that specific moment. He had to see that fire at that exact hour. All of it by divine calculation. All of it قَدَر.

And there's an even deeper layer to this qadar that connects back to an earlier instruction. Remember, Allah ﷻ told Musa's mother: أَرْضِعِيهِ - nurse him, feed him. Why mention feeding him before throwing him in the river? First, if she fed him before the soldiers walked by, the baby wouldn't cry and draw attention. Second, if she didn't feed him before putting him in the basket, he would cry during the journey down the river and someone would hear and kill him. But if she fed him, he would be relaxed, possibly asleep, and make it safely to the other shore. Third, when he arrived and someone picked him up, he would be ready for his next feeding.

Then later in the Quran, we learn: وَحَرَّمْنَا عَلَيْهِ الْمَرَاضِعَ - We made forbidden for him all wet nurses (28:12). Every woman who tried to nurse him, he refused. Why? Because Allah ﷻ had already programmed him with his mother's milk. That's why He ﷻ told her to feed him - multiple strategic reasons. One: he stays quiet during transport. Two: he doesn't arouse suspicion even at home. Three: once he arrives at the palace, he becomes accustomed only to his mother's milk. And because he's accustomed only to her milk, they will have to search for someone who can feed him. If he had accepted any other woman's milk, his mother never would have been brought to the palace. If his mother never came, he wouldn't have been raised with her influence. And if he wasn't raised knowing his true mother, his sense of identity and mission would have been completely different.

Even the feeding of that baby was part of the intricate divine plan. Every tiny detail mattered. And that's why Allah ﷻ concludes: you came exactly on schedule, Musa.

translation: And I produced you for Myself
commentary:
وَٱصْطَنَعْتُكَ لِنَفْسِى

This brief but powerful ayah is the culmination of everything Allah ﷻ has been describing. The verb اصْطَنَعْتُكَ  comes from the same root as صناعة mentioned in the previous ayah, but the form is even more intensive. The istif'al form (استفعال) indicates seeking something out with deliberateness, manufacturing something with utmost care and precision. When you add the تاء of emphasis in اصطناع, it becomes even stronger - I have manufactured you to absolute perfection, I have crafted you exactly as I intended.

Allah ﷻ is taking pride in His development of Musa (AS). He's saying: you turned out exactly as I wanted. This is precisely what I was aiming for. You are My masterpiece. The satisfaction and pleasure of Allah ﷻ in how Musa (AS) was shaped and formed radiates through this statement.

Then comes the stunning conclusion: لِنَفْسِى - for Myself. Allah ﷻ doesn't say لِأَمْرِي - for My command - or لِرِسَالَتِي - for My message. He ﷻ says "for Myself." And it's not just لِي - for Me - but لِنَفْسِى - for My own Self, exclusively. This is deeply personal, intimate language.

What does it mean that Allah ﷻ manufactured Musa (AS) for Himself? It means their relationship transcends the mission. Yes, Musa (AS) will be sent to Fir'awn, yes he will lead his people out of bondage, yes he will receive the Torah. But before all of that, he belongs to Allah ﷻ in a unique way. Allah ﷻ enjoys conversing with him, enjoys his company, takes pleasure in him as His specially crafted servant.
