This is why Musa (AS) will be called كَلِيمُ اللَّهِ - the one who spoke directly with Allah ﷻ.

The beauty of this statement is overwhelming. After recounting all the trials, the miraculous interventions, the precise calculations, the years of preparation - Allah ﷻ reveals the ultimate purpose: I made you this way for Me. Not primarily for a mission, not primarily for your people, but for Myself. You are Mine, and I take joy in you.

This is the relationship between Allah ﷻ and His chosen messenger Musa (AS) - a relationship of such intimacy and divine pleasure that it transforms how we understand prophecy itself. Before Musa (AS) delivers a message, before he confronts tyrants, before he performs miracles, he is simply someone Allah ﷻ loves, someone Allah ﷻ carefully crafted, someone who belongs to Him in a special way.

translation: Go, you and your brother, with My signs and do not slacken in My remembrance
commentary:
ٱذْهَبْ أَنتَ وَأَخُوكَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِى وَلَا تَنِيَا فِى ذِكْرِى

The conversation on Mount Sinai continues as Allah ﷻ instructs Musa (AS): "Go, you and your brother, with Our miraculous signs, and don't fall short in remembering Me." This statement carries significant weight. The word تَنِيَا  comes from the Arabic root وَنَى , which means to fall short in something, to get lazy, or to fail to fulfill your responsibilities. Allah ﷻ had just granted Musa (AS) his request for his brother Harun (AS) to accompany him as a helper. Now He's holding him accountable to that request: "I granted what you asked for. Don't fall short in that expectation."

Notice the shift in Allah's instruction. The original conversation addressed Musa (AS) in the singular form. But now that his request has been granted, the instruction shifts to the dual form—addressing both brothers together. This linguistic detail demonstrates that Allah ﷻ has already honored the request and included Harun (AS) in the mission before they even depart.

translation: Go, both of you, to Pharaoh. Indeed, he has transgressed
commentary:
ٱذْهَبَآ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُۥ طَغَىٰ

"Both of you go to Firaun. Indeed, he has transgressed." The instruction is clear and direct. The word طَغَىٰ (tagha) indicates that Firaun has rebelled beyond all bounds. He hasn't just committed minor sins—he has crossed every line of decency and morality.

translation: And speak to him with gentle speech that perhaps he may be reminded or fear [Allah]
commentary:
فَقُولَا لَهُۥ قَوْلًا لَّيِّنًا لَّعَلَّهُۥ يَتَذَكَّرُ أَوْ يَخْشَىٰ

"Then speak to him with gentle speech, perhaps he may remember or fear." This is one of the most profound instructions in the entire Quran regarding how we engage with others. The word لَّيِّنًا comes from لَيِّن, which refers to a date that is particularly soft. The grammatical form used here (صِيغَة الْمُبَالَغَة) indicates something that is consistently soft, not just temporarily so.

It's possible to start a conversation gently—"I really wanted to tell you something, I hope I don't come across as rude"—but five minutes in, you're throwing shoes at each other. That would be starting soft, but it wouldn't be لَيِّن. It wouldn't be consistently gentle. Allah ﷻ commands them to speak to Firaun with speech that remains soft throughout the entire conversation. This means watching your temper, not bringing up offensive past actions, and adjusting yourself to keep the dialogue productive.

This instruction becomes even more remarkable when we consider who they're being sent to. If there is one person on the face of the earth who doesn't deserve to be spoken to nicely, it's Firaun. Yet Allah ﷻ tells them: speak to him softly. We learn an essential lesson here about engagement. When you're dealing with someone who is truly corrupt, the instruction remains the same—don't allow your temper to rise, don't bring up past crimes, and don't say anything that will escalate the conversation.

Why this approach? لَّعَلَّهُۥ يَتَذَكَّرُ أَوْ يَخْشَىٰ—"Perhaps he may remember or he may fear Allah." The word لَعَلَّ indicates possibility. Even with someone like Firaun, who calls himself god, there's a possibility he might remember Allah ﷻ or develop some fear of Him. 

Any human being looking at Firaun's entire career would think: "Really? He's going to remember Allah? He's going to feel fear? Just because we talk to him nicely?" It seems impossible. But this connects back to an earlier ayah in the surah: يَعْلَمُ السِّرَّ وَأَخْفَىٰ—"Allah knows the secret and what is even more hidden than that." In the depths of Firaun's dark heart, there may be a spot of light somewhere that Allah ﷻ knows about, but which Musa and Harun (AS) have no right to assume exists or doesn't exist.

This is the powerful psychological mechanism of da'wah. When you speak softly, if there is any goodness in someone's heart, your words will find a way to reach it. If you speak harshly, the barriers close. The gates shut. None of your words will make their way in, and that tiny bit of goodness will never see the light of day.

If this is the case with Firaun, then what about the person praying next to you? What about Muslim youth making noise in the masjid? This attitude must permeate everything we do. It should even extend to how we talk about people—whether discussing other Muslims, governments, or nations. We excel at complaining, but acknowledging the good that exists is much harder for us. Allah ﷻ teaches us in this ayah to appreciate the possibility of goodness in a person you can't even see or imagine possessing any good.

Think about that difficult cousin, that angry uncle, that challenging aunt. Allah ﷻ is telling you: there's good in them. Speak to them nicely. Don't pass judgment. Maybe they will feel and remember.

translation: They said, "Our Lord, indeed we are afraid that he will hasten [punishment] against us or that he will transgress
commentary:
قَالَا رَبَّنَآ إِنَّنَا نَخَافُ أَن يَفْرُطَ عَلَيْنَآ أَوْ أَن يَطْغَىٰ

Both brothers respond to Allah's command with an honest expression of their fear: "Our Master, we are afraid that he will transgress against us or that he will rebel." The dual form قَالَا indicates both Musa and Harun (AS) spoke, though according to most commentators, Musa (AS) represented them both in expressing this concern.

The word يَفْرُطَ is particularly interesting. There are three related verbs in Arabic: أَفْرَطَ, فَرَّطَ, and فَرَطَ. The first means to do more than the legal limit—like crossing the line from speaking to someone to punching them. The second means to do less than the legal minimum—not even giving something a chance. The verb used here, يَفْرُطَ, encompasses both extremes. They fear that Firaun will either go overboard and start torturing or killing them, or that he won't even give them a chance to speak and will simply arrest them. Either way, he'll transgress—either by doing too much or by not allowing them any opportunity at all.

translation: [Allah] said, "Fear not. Indeed, I am with you both; I hear and I see
commentary:
قَالَ لَا تَخَافَآ ۖ إِنَّنِى مَعَكُمَآ أَسْمَعُ وَأَرَىٰ

Allah ﷻ responds with perfect reassurance: "Don't either of you fear. Indeed, I am with both of you. I hear and I see." Notice the beautiful rhetoric here—hearing is mentioned before seeing. Why? Because to hear something, you must be closer than to see it. Right now you might see a truck on the distant highway, but you can't hear it. To hear, you must be much closer. So Allah ﷻ mentions His closeness first through hearing, then His vision. He is intimately close to them, aware of everything that will unfold.

translation: So go to him and say, 'Indeed, we are messengers of your Lord, so send with us the Children of Israel and do not torment them. We have come to you with a sign from your Lord. And peace will be upon he who follows the guidance
commentary:
فَأْتِيَاهُ فَقُولَآ إِنَّا رَسُولَا رَبِّكَ فَأَرْسِلْ مَعَنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَلَا تُعَذِّبْهُمْ ۖ قَدْ جِئْنَـٰكَ بِـَٔايَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۖ وَٱلسَّلَـٰمُ عَلَىٰ مَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ ٱلْهُدَىٰٓ

"Then approach him and say: 'We are messengers of your Master. Send the Children of Israel with us and don't torture them. We have come to you with a sign from your Master. And peace be upon whoever follows guidance.'"

Allah ﷻ now provides them with the exact script for their message. Several elements stand out. First, they must identify themselves as "messengers of your Master"—not "our Master," but "your Master." This is already offensive to Firaun, who claims to be the supreme master with no one above him.

Second, they must demand the release of the enslaved Children of Israel and command him to stop torturing them. Third, they present their miraculous sign as being from "your Master"—another direct challenge to his false divinity. Fourth, the phrase وَٱلسَّلَـٰمُ عَلَىٰ مَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ ٱلْهُدَىٰٓ carries layers of meaning. On the surface, it's a greeting: "Peace be upon whoever follows guidance." But it also contains an implicit warning: the only people who will be safe, who will have peace, are those who follow guidance. If you know what's good for you, Firaun, you'll listen to us and follow this guidance, because that's the only path to safety.

translation: Indeed, it has been revealed to us that the punishment will be upon whoever denies and turns away
commentary:
إِنَّا قَدْ أُوحِىَ إِلَيْنَآ أَنَّ ٱلْعَذَابَ عَلَىٰ مَن كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّىٰ

"Indeed, it has been revealed to us that punishment will fall upon whoever denies and turns away." The message concludes with a clear warning about consequences. This is the final piece of their opening statement to Firaun.

translation: [Pharaoh] said, "So who is the Lord of you two, O Moses
commentary:
قَالَ فَمَن رَّبُّكُمَا يَـٰمُوسَىٰ

Firaun's response reveals his cunning nature: "Then who is your Master, O Musa?" Notice the sophistication of his reply. He says فَمَن رَّبُّكُمَا—"who is your Master"—addressing them in the dual form, but then singles out Musa by name: يَـٰمُوسَىٰ. He doesn't even acknowledge Harun's name, despite Harun (AS) standing right there. Why? Because Harun (AS) belongs to the enslaved class, and Firaun won't dignify him by using his name. He only addresses Musa (AS), who grew up in the palace and therefore has some standing to speak in his court.

This is Firaun's way of maintaining his hierarchy and showing subtle disrespect. "You, Musa, you have a right to speak here. But your brother? He's from the slave class. I don't need to hear from him."

translation: He said, "Our Lord is He who gave each thing its form and then guided [it]
commentary:
قَالَ رَبُّنَا ٱلَّذِىٓ أَعْطَىٰ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ خَلْقَهُۥ ثُمَّ هَدَىٰ

Musa (AS) delivers one of the most profound theological statements in the Quran: "Our Master is the One who gave everything its creation, then guided it." Notice that the response shifts from dual (قَالَا) to singular (قَالَ). Musa (AS) speaks alone now, likely signaling to Harun (AS) to remain silent so Firaun doesn't use the excuse of the "inferior" speaking to dismiss them from court.

The content of this answer is remarkable in its depth. أَعْطَىٰ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ خَلْقَهُۥ—He gave everything its creation, its very existence. Then ثُمَّ هَدَىٰ—He guided it. What does this mean? Allah ﷻ created the cow, then guided it to eat grass, not cats. It has a nature, and it won't go against that nature. He created the bee and guided it on how to build a hive. He guided pollen on what to do, guided the soil on its functions. Allah ﷻ creates, then gives purpose, direction, and guidance. These creatures—animals, insects, elements of nature—are programmed to fulfill certain roles. This is the guidance of Allah ﷻ.

The same Creator who gave everything else its nature and guidance created us. Would He then leave us without guidance? The answer embedded in Musa's response is that just as all of creation operates according to divine guidance, human beings too have been given guidance through revelation and prophets.

translation: [Pharaoh] said, "Then what is the case of the former generations
commentary:
قَالَ فَمَا بَالُ ٱلْقُرُونِ ٱلْأُولَىٰ

Firaun responds with a question designed to trap them: "Then what about the former generations?" This is a masterful deflection. Firaun is extremely intelligent, and his evil genius manifests in different ways throughout various Quranic narratives. Here, his strategy is to turn the audience against these two messengers.

They're standing in his court, surrounded by his ministers, soldiers, and staff—all listening to this conversation. He needs everyone present to hate Musa and Harun (AS). How does he accomplish this? By bringing up their ancestors. "So you say you have guidance, and your Master is the true Master. What about our fathers and their fathers and their fathers? Are you saying they're burning in hell?"

If they answer yes, everyone in the room will be offended, because this is a nation that takes immense pride in its ancestry. This isn't a sincere question seeking knowledge—it's a curveball designed to offend. The answer, no matter what it is, is meant to offend the audience.

You should recognize this tactic because people still employ it today. When discussing Islam, someone might ask: "So do you believe everyone else is going to hell? My mother was a wonderful Christian. My father was a righteous rabbi. Do you think they're burning in hell?" They're not genuinely asking about theology—they're trying to shift the conversation into uncomfortable territory where any answer seems offensive.

translation: [Moses] said, "The knowledge thereof is with my Lord in a record. My Lord neither errs nor forgets
commentary:
قَالَ عِلْمُهَا عِندَ رَبِّى فِى كِتَـٰبٍ ۖ لَّا يَضِلُّ رَبِّى وَلَا يَنسَى

Musa's response demonstrates remarkable wisdom: "Its knowledge is with my Master in a Record. My Master neither errs nor forgets." This is brilliant. He doesn't give an answer that offends everyone and causes them to stop listening. He also doesn't compromise his faith by saying something untrue like "Oh, everyone's children of God, they're all in heaven."

Instead, he gives the appropriate response: that knowledge belongs to Allah ﷻ alone. He emphasizes two qualities of Allah ﷻ: لَّا يَضِلُّ—He doesn't get confused, He doesn't make mistakes—and وَلَا يَنسَى—He doesn't forget. Every soul's fate is recorded with perfect accuracy, and Allah ﷻ will judge with perfect justice. But that judgment belongs to Him alone, not to us.

translation: [It is He] who has made for you the earth as a bed [spread out] and inserted therein for you roadways and sent down from the sky, rain and produced thereby categories of various plants
commentary:
ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ مَهْدًا وَسَلَكَ لَكُمْ فِيهَا سُبُلًا وَأَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَخْرَجْنَا بِهِۦٓ أَزْوَٰجًا مِّن نَّبَاتٍ شَتَّىٰ

Rather than getting trapped in Firaun's rhetorical game, Musa (AS) shifts the conversation to observable reality: "He is the One who made the earth a cradle for you and threaded through it pathways for you, and sent down water from the sky, and We brought forth with it pairs of diverse vegetation."

Notice how Musa (AS) redirects the discussion. Firaun wants to talk about distant ancestors and hypothetical judgments. Musa (AS) brings the conversation to the present moment: "You're asking about your forefathers who cradled you? Allah ﷻ created the original cradle—the earth itself."

The word سَلَكَ means to stretch something and extend it into something else. Allah ﷻ describes roads and pathways as Him taking pieces of earth and stretching them out to lead in various directions—valleys connecting to mountains, routes linking cities. This imagery would resonate powerfully with the Egyptians, whose civilization thrived specifically because of their strategic position at the banks of the Nile, with pathways that both facilitated trade and protected them from military attacks.

The Egyptian pharaohs built their greatest monuments right along the water so that approaching enemy states would see these colossal structures immediately upon entering their territory and become intimidated. Musa (AS) is essentially saying: "You think your civilization's power comes from your planning? Allah ﷻ created the very geography that made your civilization possible."

translation: Eat [therefrom] and pasture your livestock. Indeed, in that are signs for those of intelligence
commentary:
كُلُوا۟ وَٱرْعَوْا۟ أَنْعَـٰمَكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلنُّهَىٰ

"Eat and graze your cattle. Indeed, in all of that are signs for people who possess sound intellect." The word أُو۟لِى ٱلنُّهَىٰ refers to people with sharp, penetrating intellect—those who can follow a chain of reasoning to its ultimate conclusion.

A person with nuha looks at the sun feeding the plants through photosynthesis and water from the sky nourishing the crops, and they ask: "Who's feeding the sun? Where did the water cycle come from?" They keep tracing causes back until they reach the ultimate Cause—Allah ﷻ.

The statement carries an implicit challenge: "In all of this, there are already enough lessons. You don't need more signs from me if you really possessed sharp intellect." There's a subtle insult embedded here—"These signs weren't enough for you people, so you must not have very keen understanding."

translation: From the earth We created you, and into it We will return you, and from it We will extract you another time
commentary:
۞ مِنْهَا خَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ وَفِيهَا نُعِيدُكُمْ وَمِنْهَا نُخْرِجُكُمْ تَارَةً أُخْرَىٰ

"From it We created you, and into it We will return you, and from it We will bring you out once again." This verse establishes the cycle of human existence in relation to the earth. The word تَارَةً emphasizes repetition—doing something exactly as you did it before, like experiencing déjà vu. Allah ﷻ will bring you out from the earth on the Day of Resurrection just as He brought you forth the first time around.

translation: And We certainly showed Pharaoh Our signs - all of them - but he denied and refused
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ أَرَيْنَـٰهُ ءَايَـٰتِنَا كُلَّهَا فَكَذَّبَ وَأَبَىٰ

"And We certainly showed him all Our signs, but he denied and refused." This concluding comment about Firaun requires careful understanding. The phrase ءَايَـٰتِنَا كُلَّهَا—"all Our signs"—needs proper interpretation.

Many people approach the Quran with an overly literal mindset, especially in our age of science where everything must be taken literally and precisely. But the Quran isn't a scientific textbook—it's divine guidance that employs rich, nuanced language. Consider that Allah ﷻ describes His words by saying that if all the oceans were turned into ink, they would run dry before His words were exhausted. Even if you added more oceans, still Allah's words would not come to an end.

So when the ayah says Allah ﷻ showed Firaun "all" the signs, it doesn't mean literally every single sign that could possibly exist. It means all the signs he needed to see, a comprehensive array of signs sufficient for guidance. It's like when a husband and wife are arguing and the husband says, "I did everything for you. I gave you everything." Does that mean literally everything in existence? No, it means he provided abundantly, everything that should reasonably matter.

The Quran is full of such expressions—they're figures of speech that convey meaning powerfully without demanding wooden literalism. Despite being shown abundant, overwhelming evidence, Firaun فَكَذَّبَ—he lied against these signs, denying their truth—وَأَبَىٰ—and he refused to accept them. Both verbs emphasize his willful, obstinate rejection of clarity.

translation: He said, "Have you come to us to drive us out of our land with your magic, O Moses
commentary:
قَالَ أَجِئْتَنَا لِتُخْرِجَنَا مِنْ أَرْضِنَا بِسِحْرِكَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ

Fir'awn's response reveals his cunning manipulation of public sentiment. Notice the brilliance in his words: "Did you come to us to get us out of our land?" He doesn't say "my land" or "me" - though Fir'awn is an arrogant king who typically speaks only of himself. Usually he declares, "Don't I alone own the dominion of Egypt? Don't these rivers flow under me?" But here he strategically uses "us" and "our" to rally the entire nation against Musa (AS). He wants the crowd unified against this perceived threat.

By framing Musa's mission as an attempt to expel them from their homeland through magic, Fir'awn transforms a spiritual confrontation into a national security crisis. He's painting Musa (AS) not as a prophet calling to truth, but as a foreign aggressor using supernatural means to overthrow their society.

translation: Then we will surely bring you magic like it, so make between us and you an appointment, which we will not fail to keep and neither will you, in a place assigned
commentary:
فَلَنَأْتِيَنَّكَ بِسِحْرٍ مِّثْلِهِۦ فَٱجْعَلْ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكَ مَوْعِدًا لَّا نُخْلِفُهُۥ نَحْنُ وَلَآ أَنتَ مَكَانًا سُوًى

Fir'awn continues: "Then we will bring about a magic just like yours, equivalent to yours." He's projecting confidence to his people, showing them he has a plan to counter this threat. Then comes his proposal for a public showdown: "Let's set an appointment between you an d us."

Notice his arrogance even in this challenge - when he says "we won't go back on it, nor will you," he mentions himself first. Despite using collective language to manipulate the crowd, his ego cannot help but place himself at the forefront.

The phrase مَكَانًا سُوًى (a level place) carries multiple meanings. Physically, it refers to an even, flat terrain where large crowds can gather and observe from all directions - like the town squares where festivals and major announcements took place, with buildings surrounding the central area so people could watch from their homes and balconies. The more level the ground, the farther people could see the spectacle.

But سُوًى also suggests fairness and balance - a neutral ground where neither party has an advantage. Fir'awn is posturing as though he's being fair, though the irony is inescapable: this is Egypt, his kingdom. Every location is inherently unfair because he controls everything - the soldiers, the population, the very land itself.

translation: [Moses] said, "Your appointment is on the day of the festival when the people assemble at mid-morning
commentary:
قَالَ مَوْعِدُكُمْ يَوْمُ ٱلزِّينَةِ وَأَن يُحْشَرَ ٱلنَّاسُ ضُحًى

Musa (AS) seizes this opportunity brilliantly. Fir'awn told him to pick the day, so he chooses the most public day possible: يَوْمُ ٱلزِّينَةِ - the Day of Beauty, the day of the festival. This is their national celebration, when the streets are decorated with floats, flags and festivities. It's like choosing July Fourth or Memorial Day - the day when people come out anyway, when the nation is already gathered.

Musa (AS) wants this to be a public spectacle because the truth has nothing to hide. He even specifies the time: ضُحًى - the mid-morning, around the time of the Duha prayer. This is strategic timing in Egypt's climate - early enough that the heat isn't unbearable, comfortable enough for crowds to gather and remain for hours. Musa (AS) is doing event management, ensuring maximum attendance for this demonstration of Allah's power.

translation: So Pharaoh went away, put together his plan, and then came [to Moses]
commentary:
فَتَوَلَّىٰ فِرْعَوْنُ فَجَمَعَ كَيْدَهُۥ ثُمَّ أَتَىٰ

Fir'awn turned away - the meeting adjourned, and each party went their separate ways to prepare. The phrase فَجَمَعَ كَيْدَهُۥ (he gathered all his plot) encompasses everything: recruiting magicians from across the empire, training them, coordinating their strategy. Other passages in the Quran provide more details about how he assembled and prepared this legion - some narrations mention as many as ten thousand magicians, arranged in rows upon rows.

ثُمَّ أَتَىٰ - Then he came, ready for confrontation.

translation: Moses said to the magicians summoned by Pharaoh, "Woe to you! Do not invent a lie against Allah or He will exterminate you with a punishment; and he has failed who invents [such falsehood]
commentary:
قَالَ لَهُم مُّوسَىٰ وَيْلَكُمْ لَا تَفْتَرُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا فَيُسْحِتَكُم بِعَذَابٍ ۖ وَقَدْ خَابَ مَنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ

Before the contest begins, Musa (AS) addresses the magicians directly with a powerful warning: وَيْلَكُمْ - "Woe unto you" - a curse, a plea for them to recognize the gravity of what they're about to do. "Don't fabricate lies against Allah ﷻ."

The consequence he warns of is devastating: فَيُسْحِتَكُم بِعَذَابٍ - "He will destroy you with punishment." The word يُسْحِت comes from سُحْت, which refers to corrupt wealth - money earned through bribery, forbidden transactions, or selling prohibited items. It also means the destruction that comes when something is neglected: crops left unharvested, buildings unmaintained, gardens unwatered. Everything deteriorates and is destroyed.

This word choice is prophetic. When Fir'awn and his wealthy army are eventually drowned, their estates, farms and homes will have no one to maintain them. Everything will fall into ruin and decay - a complete destruction of their material world.

وَقَدْ خَابَ مَنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ - "And the one who fabricates has certainly failed." The word خَابَ describes someone in the worst possible state, with all hope destroyed, unable to sink any lower. Musa (AS) is warning them that manufacturing lies against Allah ﷻ leads to utter wretchedness.

translation: So they disputed over their affair among themselves and concealed their private conversation
commentary:
فَتَنَـٰزَعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَهُم بَيْنَهُمْ وَأَسَرُّوا۟ ٱلنَّجْوَىٰ

This powerful speech from Musa (AS) shook them. The magicians and Fir'awn's administration began having internal debates - this is before the actual day of the festival, during their preparation and cabinet meetings. فَتَنَـٰزَعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَهُم بَيْنَهُمْ - they disagreed among themselves about how to proceed. 

Some were uncertain: "Should we really fight against Musa? What is he talking about? What is this 'Allah' he mentions? I'm scared. Maybe we shouldn't do this." There were different opinions, different levels of confidence within the administration.

وَأَسَرُّوا۟ ٱلنَّجْوَىٰ - They kept their private counsel secret. These internal debates and doubts were not for public consumption. They needed to present a unified front.

translation: They said, "Indeed, these are two magicians who want to drive you out of your land with their magic and do away with your most exemplary way
commentary:
قَالُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَٰنِ لَسَـٰحِرَٰنِ يُرِيدَانِ أَن يُخْرِجَاكُم مِّنْ أَرْضِكُم بِسِحْرِهِمَا وَيَذْهَبَا بِطَرِيقَتِكُمُ ٱلْمُثْلَىٰ

After their private disagreements, they emerged with their official public messaging: "These two are absolutely magicians." The particle إِنْ combined with لَ creates emphasis - this is their definitive propaganda.

Notice they now mention both Musa and Harun (AS) - يُرِيدَانِ أَن يُخْرِجَاكُم - "They want to expel you from your land through their magic." The message mirrors Fir'awn's original framing: this is about national security, about protecting the homeland.

But then comes the most insidious part of their propaganda: وَيَذْهَبَا بِطَرِيقَتِكُمُ ٱلْمُثْلَىٰ - "And they want to eliminate your exemplary way of life." 

Let's examine this phrase. الطَّرِيقَةُ الْمُثْلَىٰ literally means "the exemplary method" or "the model lifestyle." المُثْلَىٰ comes from مَثَل - an example that is repeatedly given, a standard held up for emulation. This is when your mother says, "Why can't you be like your sister?" Or when people say, "That country is amazing - look at their infrastructure, their technology, their administration."

Fir'awn's administration wanted to spread the idea that their civilization was perfect, that nations around the world looked to Egypt as the model to emulate. There was an element of truth in this - kings across the ancient world did try to imitate the Pharaohs. Chinese rulers built castles mimicking Egyptian architecture. The Greeks and Romans modeled their monuments after Egyptian designs. Egypt was genuinely seen as a pinnacle of civilization.

So the propaganda was: "We have a great nation. Other peoples want to be like us. We are the example of greatness. And this man Musa is a threat to our superior way of life, to our national identity." 

The message was clear: if you love your country, you must hate Musa. If you value your civilization, you must oppose him. They wanted to make opposition to Musa (AS) synonymous with patriotism.

translation: So resolve upon your plan and then come [forward] in line. And he has succeeded today who overcomes
commentary:
فَأَجْمِعُوا۟ كَيْدَكُمْ ثُمَّ ٱئْتُوا۟ صَفًّا ۚ وَقَدْ أَفْلَحَ ٱلْيَوْمَ مَنِ ٱسْتَعْلَىٰ

With the propaganda established, now comes the pep talk to the magicians: فَأَجْمِعُوا۟ كَيْدَكُمْ - "Consolidate all your plots, be unified in your strategy." They needed to combine their skills, coordinate their efforts, work as one cohesive unit.

ثُمَّ ٱئْتُوا۟ صَفًّا - "Then come in rows," presenting a united, disciplined front before the masses.

وَقَدْ أَفْلَحَ ٱلْيَوْمَ مَنِ ٱسْتَعْلَىٰ - "And today, success belongs to whoever achieves supremacy." The word ٱسْتَعْلَىٰ means to rise above, to be on top, to dominate. They're telling the magicians: today is about impressing the crowd, about demonstrating superiority. Whoever wins the hearts and minds of the people wins everything.

The magicians are now fired up, ready to save their nation from this perceived threat.

translation: They said, "O Moses, either you throw or we will be the first to throw
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِمَّآ أَن تُلْقِىَ وَإِمَّآ أَن نَّكُونَ أَوَّلَ مَنْ أَلْقَىٰ

The confident magicians address Musa (AS): "O Musa, either you throw first, or we'll be the first to throw." They're giving him the choice, perhaps as a show of sportsmanship, or perhaps trying to gain a psychological advantage by observing his move first.

translation: He said, "Rather, you throw." And suddenly their ropes and staffs seemed to him from their magic that they were moving [like snakes]
commentary:
قَالَ بَلْ أَلْقُوا۟ ۖ فَإِذَا حِبَالُهُمْ وَعِصِيُّهُمْ يُخَيَّلُ إِلَيْهِ مِن سِحْرِهِمْ أَنَّهَا تَسْعَىٰ

Musa (AS) responds: بَلْ أَلْقُوا۟ - "No, you throw first." He has nothing to hide, no tricks that require him to go first.

What happens next is remarkable: فَإِذَا حِبَالُهُمْ وَعِصِيُّهُمْ - their ropes and their staffs - يُخَيَّلُ إِلَيْهِ مِن سِحْرِهِمْ أَنَّهَا تَسْعَىٰ - they appeared to him, because of their magic, as though they were slithering about.

Understand the context: after Musa's first encounter with Fir'awn, the king had asked his magicians, "What happened? How did he embarrass you?" They explained, "He threw a staff and it turned into a serpent. It was overwhelming." Fir'awn said, "We can work with that." They spent all this time practicing how to make ropes and rods appear as serpents. They perfected their illusion to such a degree that even Musa (AS), when he saw it, witnessed what looked like hundreds of snakes moving across the ground.

This was Musa's planned grand finale - throwing his staff to have it transform into a serpent. But now there were hundreds of staffs appearing to turn into serpents. How would his one miracle stand out against this spectacle?

translation: And he sensed within himself apprehension, did Moses
commentary:
فَأَوْجَسَ فِى نَفْسِهِۦ خِيفَةً مُّوسَىٰ

This moment reveals Musa's humanity: فَأَوْجَسَ فِى نَفْسِهِۦ خِيفَةً - he sensed within himself a hidden fear. The word أَوْجَسَ means to feel something and keep it concealed within, to harbor a fear without letting it show externally.

Musa (AS) thought to himself, "That was supposed to be my decisive demonstration. But now there are hundreds of serpents. How will mine make any impression?" The fear was there, deep inside, but he maintained his composure outwardly. This is the reality of prophets - they're human, they experience fear and uncertainty, but they trust in Allah ﷻ.

translation: Allah said, "Fear not. Indeed, it is you who are superior
commentary:
قُلْنَا لَا تَخَفْ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ

At this critical moment, Allah ﷻ reassures His prophet: "Don't be afraid. You - you are the one who will be supreme." إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ - You will rise above. This echoes the very word used by Fir'awn's administration - ٱسْتَعْلَىٰ - but now Allah ﷻ is declaring who will truly achieve supremacy.

translation: And throw what is in your right hand; it will swallow up what they have crafted. What they have crafted is but the trick of a magician, and the magician will not succeed wherever he is
commentary:
وَأَلْقِ مَا فِى يَمِينِكَ تَلْقَفْ مَا صَنَعُوٓا۟ ۖ إِنَّمَا صَنَعُوا۟ كَيْدُ سَـٰحِرٍ ۖ وَلَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلسَّاحِرُ حَيْثُ أَتَىٰ

The command comes: وَأَلْقِ مَا فِى يَمِينِكَ - "Throw what is in your right hand." Allah ﷻ doesn't even call it a staff anymore - compared to their elaborate display, it seems insignificant. Just throw whatever that thing is in your hand.

تَلْقَفْ مَا صَنَعُوٓا۟ - "It will swallow what they have made." The word تَلْقَفْ is significant - it means to swallow something quickly, skillfully, with precision. Like a predator's strike - a shark's attack, an alligator's bite - swift, decisive, complete. This won't be a slow consumption; it will be an immediate, masterful devouring.

Notice how Allah ﷻ describes their work: مَا صَنَعُوٓا۟ - "what they manufactured" or "what they produced." This word choice indicates extensive preparation, rehearsals, hours upon hours of practice to perfect this trick. They built this illusion carefully.

إِنَّمَا صَنَعُوا۟ كَيْدُ سَـٰحِرٍ - "What they have produced is merely the plot of a magician." It's just tricks, just deception, nothing more.

وَلَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلسَّاحِرُ حَيْثُ أَتَىٰ - "And the magician will never succeed wherever he goes." No matter what tricks he employs, no matter how elaborate his schemes, the magician cannot achieve true success because his work is built on falsehood.

translation: So the magicians fell down in prostration. They said, "We have believed in the Lord of Aaron and Moses
commentary:
فَأُلْقِىَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ سُجَّدًا قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِرَبِّ هَـٰرُونَ وَمُوسَىٰ

Now we must understand the context of what the crowd witnessed. There were multiple groups watching: Musa (AS) and the believers with him, the magicians, Fir'awn and his ministers, and hundreds of thousands in the general public. But there was only one group besides the believers who understood what actually happened - the magicians themselves.

To everyone else, this looked like Musa's magic simply overpowered their magic. His serpent ate their serpents. He wins. He's the best magician. That's what Fir'awn will later use: "He's your master magician who taught you."

But the magicians knew different. They were experts in their craft. They understood the limits of magic, the boundaries of what human skill could achieve. They had brought together the dream team - thousands of magicians from across the empire, collaborating, combining their knowledge and abilities. This wasn't the work of one illusionist; this was the collective effort of the greatest practitioners of magic in the known world.

And in one moment, it was all destroyed. Not by better magic, but by something fundamentally different. They had created illusions that looked like serpents. But Musa's staff became an actual serpent - you could touch it, see its fangs, watch it devour their ropes and rods. They recognized instantly: this transcends the limits of our knowledge. This is not magic. This is divine.

When you're a specialist in any field - science, medicine, engineering - you know how far your discipline can take you. When you witness something that clearly defies those limits, you recognize there's something greater at work.

فَأُلْقِىَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ سُجَّدًا - The magicians were thrown down into prostration. The passive voice indicates they had no control - they were overwhelmed, compelled by what they witnessed to fall in submission before Allah ﷻ.

قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِرَبِّ هَـٰرُونَ وَمُوسَىٰ - "We believe in the Lord of Harun and Musa." 

Notice whose name comes first here: Harun, then Musa. Throughout this story, the focus has been on Musa (AS) - this passage highlights his role, his confrontation, his miracle. But now, in this declaration of faith, Harun is mentioned first as a deliberate acknowledgment of both messengers.

This public prostration, this public declaration of faith, was the worst possible outcome for Fir'awn. He had orchestrated this as a grand spectacle to humiliate Musa (AS) and rally the nation behind him. Instead, his own champions, his elite magicians, fell prostrate before the God of Musa (AS) in front of hundreds of thousands of witnesses. The very public nature of this event - which Fir'awn had insisted upon - became the means of his greatest humiliation.

translation: [Pharaoh] said, "You believed him before I gave you permission. Indeed, he is your leader who has taught you magic. So I will surely cut off your hands and your feet on opposite sides, and I will crucify you on the trunks of palm trees, and you will surely know which of us is more severe in [giving] punishment and more enduring
commentary:
قَالَ ءَامَنتُمْ لَهُۥ قَبْلَ أَنْ ءَاذَنَ لَكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَكَبِيرُكُمُ ٱلَّذِى عَلَّمَكُمُ ٱلسِّحْرَ ۖ فَلَأُقَطِّعَنَّ أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَرْجُلَكُم مِّنْ خِلَـٰفٍ وَلَأُصَلِّبَنَّكُمْ فِى جُذُوعِ ٱلنَّخْلِ وَلَتَعْلَمُنَّ أَيُّنَآ أَشَدُّ عَذَابًا وَأَبْقَىٰ

Fir'awn's rage and desperation manifest in this ayah as he confronts the magicians who have just declared their faith. His response reveals the tactics of tyrants when faced with truth they cannot suppress.

He begins with an accusation: "You believed in him before I gave you permission?" This statement exposes the heart of tyranny—the assumption that even faith requires the ruler's authorization. Then he attempts to undermine the miracle by claiming it was all orchestrated: "He's your chief, the one who taught you magic to begin with." This desperate conspiracy theory attempts to explain away what cannot be rationally denied.

When manipulation fails, Fir'awn resorts to brutal threats. The punishment he describes is horrifyingly specific: "I will chop off your hands and feet from opposite ends"—right hand with left foot, left hand with right foot. This mutilation is followed by an even more gruesome fate: "Then I will crucify you, nail you to the trunks of trees." The word الصلب (crucifixion) carries the meaning of literally nailing the body through, not merely hanging. This was designed to be a prolonged, agonizing death and a public spectacle of terror.

His final threat contains bitter irony: "Soon you will come to learn which of us is more powerful in delivering punishment and who lasts longer." The magicians had just witnessed eternal truth, while Fir'awn speaks only of temporal power. He cannot see that his authority extends only over this brief worldly life, while they have glimpsed the eternal.

translation: They said, "Never will we prefer you over what has come to us of clear proofs and [over] He who created us. So decree whatever you are to decree. You can only decree for this worldly life
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ لَن نُّؤْثِرَكَ عَلَىٰ مَا جَآءَنَا مِنَ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَٱلَّذِى فَطَرَنَا ۖ فَٱقْضِ مَآ أَنتَ قَاضٍ ۖ إِنَّمَا تَقْضِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَآ

The magicians' response is one of the most powerful declarations of faith in the Quran. "We won't give you preference anymore," they announce. The word نُؤْثِرَ  means to give precedence or preference—Fir'awn no longer has any priority in their hearts or considerations.

They explain their resolve with crystal clarity: they cannot prefer him "over what has come to us from the clearest possible proofs and from the One who created us." Notice the progression: they mention the بَيِّنَات (clear proofs) they witnessed, then immediately connect to الَّذِى فَطَرَنَا (the One who created us). In one transformative moment, they moved from recognizing divine power to recognizing their Creator and their relationship with Him.

Their next words drip with dignified defiance: "Go ahead and pass your verdict, whatever verdict you're going to pass." Then comes the devastating truth that strips Fir'awn of all his pretensions: "All you can pass verdicts on is this worldly life." Your jurisdiction, they tell him, extends only to هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَآ (this worldly life). You can touch our bodies, but you have no authority over our souls, our faith, or our eternal destiny.

This is their speech in public, before thousands of people. The scene is remarkable—one magician speaks and soldiers grab him, another begins speaking and more soldiers seize him, a third continues the declaration. They simply cannot be silenced. The truth has set them free, and no earthly power can take that freedom away.

translation: Indeed, we have believed in our Lord that He may forgive us our sins and what you compelled us [to do] of magic. And Allah is better and more enduring
commentary:
إِنَّآ ءَامَنَّا بِرَبِّنَا لِيَغْفِرَ لَنَا خَطَـٰيَـٰنَا وَمَآ أَكْرَهْتَنَا عَلَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلسِّحْرِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقَىٰ

The magicians articulate their understanding of what faith means: "We have come to believe in our Master so that He may forgive our mistakes." They immediately grasp that belief opens the door to divine forgiveness. The word خَطَـٰيَـٰنَا (our mistakes) acknowledges their past errors.

Then they add a profound detail: "and whatever you forced us to do from magic." This reveals that originally, they hadn't wanted to participate in this confrontation. They were soldiers' conscripts, forcibly brought to perform before Fir'awn. This context makes their transformation even more striking—they came under compulsion but left as willing believers.

Their final statement directly challenges Fir'awn's earlier threat. He had said, "you will learn which of us is more severe in punishment and more lasting." They respond: "And Allah is خَيْرٌ (better, more good) and أَبْقَىٰ (more lasting, more eternal)." They deliberately echo his words, but shift from his focus on punishment to Allah's ﷻ goodness, and from temporary worldly power to eternal divine authority.

## Ayahs 74-76

إِنَّهُۥ مَن يَأْتِ رَبَّهُۥ مُجْرِمًا فَإِنَّ لَهُۥ جَهَنَّمَ لَا يَمُوتُ فِيهَا وَلَا يَحْيَىٰ

وَمَن يَأْتِهِۦ مُؤْمِنًا قَدْ عَمِلَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمُ ٱلدَّرَجَـٰتُ ٱلْعُلَىٰ

جَنَّـٰتُ عَدْنٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ مَن تَزَكَّىٰ

The magicians continue their profound theological discourse, now addressing the reality of the Hereafter. They use the word مُجْرِمًا (as a criminal) with deliberate irony. At this very moment, Fir'awn has declared them criminals deserving execution. But they flip the script: the real criminals are those who come before Allah ﷻ in a state of rejection and sin.

The description of Jahannam is haunting: "He won't live in it and he won't die in it." This captures the unique torment of hellfire—a state of perpetual suffering where death would be a mercy, but death never comes. It's an existence suspended between life and death, experiencing the worst of both.

Then they paint the contrast: "Whoever comes to Him as a believer that has done some righteous deeds." Notice they don't say "whoever comes as a believer" and stop there. Belief alone isn't enough—it must be accompanied by عَمِلَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ (righteous actions). What righteous deeds have these magicians already performed? They fell into prostration and declared their faith before a hostile crowd—these themselves are tremendous good deeds. Declaring faith in the face of death threats is among the highest forms of righteousness.

Such believers "will have الدَّرَجَـٰتُ ٱلْعُلَىٰ (the highest levels)." This word is particularly meaningful because Fir'awn had been obsessed with levels and stations—he had the highest level in society, the highest throne, the highest status. Now the magicians speak of true elevation—spiritual ranks in Paradise.

They describe these rewards as جَنَّـٰتُ عَدْنٍ (Gardens of Eden)—but how do these magicians know about the Gardens of Eden? Just minutes ago, they were opposing Musa (AS). The answer reveals something profound: they had been studying their enemy. They attended his speeches, listened to his message, learned about Allah ﷻ, about Paradise, about accountability. They knew about it intellectually, but they didn't believe it.

This teaches us a crucial lesson: you can learn about Islam and not have faith. You can know all about your religion and not believe in it. Just because knowledge is in your head doesn't mean it has entered your heart. It was in their heads all this time—now, finally, it entered their hearts. This is why they can articulate these truths so eloquently in their moment of transformation.

They conclude: "That is the compensation of whoever purifies themselves." The word تَزَكَّىٰ means to purify oneself. They now recognize that magic was spiritual filth, that serving Fir'awn was corruption of the soul. Musa (AS) had told them this before, but now they truly understand. Purification means removing the corruption of shirk, the pollution of serving false gods, and cleansing the heart for the worship of Allah ﷻ alone.

translation: Indeed, whoever comes to his Lord as a criminal - indeed, for him is Hell; he will neither die therein nor live
commentary:
إِنَّهُۥ مَن يَأْتِ رَبَّهُۥ مُجْرِمًا فَإِنَّ لَهُۥ جَهَنَّمَ لَا يَمُوتُ فِيهَا وَلَا يَحْيَىٰ

*Indeed, whoever comes to his Lord as a criminal, then surely for him is Hell. He will neither die therein nor truly live.*

After the magicians publicly declared their faith and accepted whatever punishment Fir'awn might impose upon them, they immediately shifted their attention from the threats of this world to the realities of the next. Fir'awn could torture their bodies, but he could not control their final destination. Having tasted faith, they now viewed everything through the lens of the Hereafter.

The word مُجْرِمًا refers to one who commits crimes against Allah ﷻ and knowingly rebels against the truth. Its root carries the meaning of cutting away. A criminal cuts himself away from guidance, mercy and obedience. The magicians understood that the real danger was not losing one's life but losing one's relationship with Allah.

Allah ﷻ then describes one of the most terrifying realities in the Quran: Hell, where a person neither dies nor lives. Death would bring relief, but death does not come. Life would imply comfort, joy or hope, but none of those exist. The inhabitants remain trapped between the two. They continue to exist while being deprived of everything that makes existence meaningful.

This description teaches that the punishment of the Hereafter is unlike any suffering in this world. Even the most difficult worldly pain eventually ends through recovery, escape or death. The punishment of Hell contains none of those exits. It is a consequence designed by the Creator who fully knows the reality of the human soul.

The magicians mention this reality because they have reached certainty. Only moments earlier they were servants of Fir'awn. Now they have become servants of Allah ﷻ. Their priorities have changed completely. They no longer fear what Fir'awn can do to them because they fear something far greater: appearing before Allah as a criminal.

translation: But whoever comes to Him as a believer having done righteous deeds - for those will be the highest degrees
commentary:
وَمَن يَأْتِهِۦ مُؤْمِنًا قَدْ عَمِلَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمُ ٱلدَّرَجَـٰتُ ٱلْعُلَىٰ

*But whoever comes to Him as a believer having performed righteous deeds, then for such people are the highest ranks.*

The Quran consistently balances warning with hope. After mentioning the fate of criminals, Allah ﷻ immediately turns to the reward of the believers. Faith is not merely belief in the heart. It manifests itself through righteous action. For this reason Allah ﷻ joins faith and good deeds together repeatedly throughout the Quran.

The phrase ٱلدَّرَجَـٰتُ ٱلْعُلَىٰ refers to the highest levels and ranks. Paradise is not a single undifferentiated place. It contains degrees, each reflecting the faith, sincerity and sacrifice of those who enter it. Just as people differ in this world, they will differ in rank in the Hereafter.

Notice the contrast with Fir'awn. He desired the highest rank in this world. He demanded that people see him as supreme. Yet Allah ﷻ teaches that true elevation belongs only to those who humble themselves before Him. The magicians lost their worldly status but gained something infinitely greater.

This ayah also teaches that Allah's rewards are not limited by worldly standards. Some people live short lives. Others possess little wealth or influence. Yet through sincere faith and righteous deeds, they can attain the highest stations in Paradise. The scales of the Hereafter measure different realities than the scales of this world.

translation: Gardens of perpetual residence beneath which rivers flow, wherein they will abide forever; and that is the reward of one who purifies himself
commentary:
جَنَّـٰتُ عَدْنٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ مَن تَزَكَّىٰ

*Gardens of everlasting residence beneath which rivers flow, abiding therein forever. And that is the reward of the one who purified himself.*

Allah ﷻ now describes the destination awaiting those believers. The expression جَنَّاتُ عَدْنٍ refers to gardens of permanent settlement. In worldly life every home is temporary. People move, travel and eventually leave everything behind. Paradise is the first true home from which no departure will ever occur.

The rivers flowing beneath the gardens symbolize abundance, beauty and constant renewal. Unlike worldly blessings, which diminish with time, the blessings of Paradise remain fresh and perfect forever.

The ayah concludes with a powerful statement: وَذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ مَن تَزَكَّىٰ – 'That is the reward of the one who purified himself.' The word تَزَكَّىٰ combines two meanings. It refers to purification and growth. Just as a farmer removes weeds so that a plant may flourish, the believer removes sins, arrogance and corruption from his heart so that faith can grow.

This is precisely what happened to the magicians. They purified themselves from shirk and submission to Fir'awn. In a matter of moments their hearts were transformed. The lesson is profound: guidance can arrive suddenly, but once it arrives, it demands purification, sincerity and commitment. Those who respond in that way are promised eternal gardens and everlasting success.

translation: And We had inspired to Moses, "Travel by night with My servants and strike for them a dry path through the sea; you will not fear being overtaken [by Pharaoh] nor be afraid [of drowning]
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰٓ أَنْ أَسْرِ بِعِبَادِى فَٱضْرِبْ لَهُمْ طَرِيقًا فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ يَبَسًا لَّا تَخَـٰفُ دَرَكًا وَلَا تَخْشَىٰ

The narrative now shifts to the escape from Egypt. Allah ﷻ says, "We had revealed to Musa: take My slaves in the middle of the night." The command is أَسْرِ—travel by night, move under cover of darkness. Then comes the instruction that would require absolute faith: "Strike for them a path in the ocean."

When Musa (AS) reaches the shore with hundreds of thousands of Bani Israel behind him and Fir'awn's armies approaching, he is to strike his staff. The sea will part, creating a path that is يَبَسًا —completely dry. Not muddy, not damp, but dry earth at the bottom of the ocean.

Allah ﷻ addresses two fears that Musa (AS) might experience: "You won't have to be afraid of being caught from behind." The word دَرَكًا means being overtaken or caught—whether by Fir'awn's armies pursuing from behind or, metaphorically, being caught by the reality of being at the bottom of the ocean. Imagine the psychological weight: you're walking at the ocean floor, and on either side, water stands like mountains. Other passages in the Quran describe this water as clear—you can see through it. Marine life, sharks, whales, all visible through the transparent walls of water. It's a terrifying sight.

The second assurance: وَلَا تَخْشَىٰ (and you won't be overwhelmed by fear). The word خَشْيَة  describes a paralyzing, overwhelming fear. Someone might start walking but halfway through become so terrified they cannot continue. Allah ﷻ promises that neither Musa (AS) nor those following him will be paralyzed by this natural fear of their impossible circumstances.

Both types of fear—the practical and the psychological—are mentioned in the same ayah, highlighting their distinct natures. It takes special divine assurance to walk calmly at the bottom of a parted ocean.

translation: So Pharaoh pursued them with his soldiers, and there covered them from the sea that which covered them
commentary:
فَأَتْبَعَهُمْ فِرْعَوْنُ بِجُنُودِهِۦ فَغَشِيَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْيَمِّ مَا غَشِيَهُمْ

Fir'awn, seeing the miracle but blinded by arrogance, "followed right behind them with his armies." The word أَتْبَعَ (atba'a) indicates he pursued them persistently, entering the same path through the sea.

Then comes the devastating consequence: فَغَشِيَهُم (it covered them). The word غَشِيَ means to cover but not to make completely invisible. This is significant—the drowning wasn't a complete concealment. You could still see them being overtaken by the water, see the spears and shields floating, witness the destruction unfolding. The repetition "what covered them covered them"  emphasizes the totality and horror of that covering—the massive waves crashing from both sides, crushing the army between walls of water.

The restraint in description is eloquent. Allah ﷻ doesn't elaborate on the gruesome details. The simple phrase "from the ocean, it covered them—whatever it was that covered them" leaves the scene to our imagination while conveying its catastrophic nature.

translation: And Pharaoh led his people astray and did not guide [them]
commentary:
وَأَضَلَّ فِرْعَوْنُ قَوْمَهُۥ وَمَا هَدَىٰ

This ayah serves as both literal description and spiritual commentary: "And Fir'awn misguided his nation, and he didn't guide them." 

On the literal level, he misguided them to their destruction—leading them into the sea where they drowned. He literally led them to the wrong path, down into the ocean floor where they perished.

On the spiritual level, this summarizes his entire reign. Throughout his rule, he misguided his people away from truth, away from the worship of Allah ﷻ, away from recognizing the prophethood of Musa (AS). He never guided them toward what was right.

This is the beautiful dual nature of many expressions in this Surah—words that work simultaneously on both the physical and metaphorical planes. Fir'awn led his people to fall, both literally (into the drowning waters) and figuratively (off the path of truth and guidance).

translation: O Children of Israel, We delivered you from your enemy, and We made an appointment with you at the right side of the mount, and We sent down to you manna and quails
commentary:
يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ قَدْ أَنجَيْنَـٰكُم مِّنْ عَدُوِّكُمْ وَوَٰعَدْنَـٰكُمْ جَانِبَ ٱلطُّورِ ٱلْأَيْمَنَ وَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْمَنَّ وَٱلسَّلْوَىٰ

Now Allah ﷻ addresses Bani Israel directly. They've crossed to the other side and been saved. The address shifts from speaking about them to speaking to them, because the Bani Israel were among the primary audience of this Surah. The Muslims learning about Musa (AS) for the first time are one audience, but the Jews listening to these revelations are thinking, "How does he know all these details about Musa's personal life, his mother, his sister, the drowning of Fir'awn?" The level of detail here is striking.

Allah ﷻ reminds them: "We have rescued you from your enemy." Then: "We have taken a promise from you from the right side of Mount Tur." This is جَانِبَ ٱلطُّورِ ٱلْأَيْمَنَ (the right side of the mountain of Tur), the same geographical location where Allah ﷻ spoke directly with Musa (AS)—a location that will be described in more detail later in the Quran.

"And We sent down upon you المَنَّ and السَّلْوَىٰ"—miraculous provisions that would appear each morning. These blessings are listed to remind them of the continuous divine care they experienced.

translation: [Saying], "Eat from the good things with which We have provided you and do not transgress [or oppress others] therein, lest My anger should descend upon you. And he upon whom My anger descends has certainly fallen
commentary:
كُلُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا رَزَقْنَـٰكُمْ وَلَا تَطْغَوْا۟ فِيهِ فَيَحِلَّ عَلَيْكُمْ غَضَبِى ۖ وَمَن يَحْلِلْ عَلَيْهِ غَضَبِى فَقَدْ هَوَىٰ

The command comes with the provision: "Consume from the good and pure things We've provided you." The word الطَّيِّبَات emphasizes both the quality and the permissibility of these provisions.

But then comes the warning: وَلَا تَطْغَوْا۟ فِيهِ (and don't rebel in it, don't go overboard in it). What does rebellion look like with free food appearing every morning? It means hoarding when Allah ﷻ said not to store it. It means taking someone else's portion. It means accumulating it out of mistrust, thinking, "Who knows if it will show up tomorrow morning?" These are forms of corruption and rebellion against divine instruction.

The consequence is severe: فَيَحِلَّ عَلَيْكُمْ غَضَبِى (My anger will open up against you). The word يَحِلَّ literally means to become halal or permissible, but in this context, it means the doors to divine anger will flood open. It's as if Allah's ﷻ anger was held back by gates, and those gates will swing wide.

Then comes a terrifying description: "And whoever My anger opens up against, فَقَدْ هَوَىٰ (he has certainly fallen)." The word هَوَىٰ is used elsewhere in the Quran: "when the star falls" (وَالنَّجْمِ إِذَا هَوَىٰ). It describes something falling from a tremendous height and being completely crushed upon impact. Earlier in this Surah, we read about falling off a cliff. Now this is even higher—this person has fallen from such an elevation that they are utterly destroyed, completely wrecked.

translation: But indeed, I am the Perpetual Forgiver of whoever repents and believes and does righteousness and then continues in guidance
commentary:
وَإِنِّى لَغَفَّارٌ لِّمَن تَابَ وَءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا ثُمَّ ٱهْتَدَىٰ

But immediately after the warning comes divine mercy: "And I am extremely forgiving." The word غَفَّارٌ is an intensive form—it emphasizes Allah's ﷻ abundant, overwhelming capacity for forgiveness.

But forgiveness isn't automatic. It requires four conditions, mentioned in sequence:

First, تَابَ - repentance. Turning back from the wrong path.

Second, وَءَامَنَ - and believed. Faith must accompany repentance.

Third, وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا - and acted righteously. Faith must manifest in action.

Fourth, ثُمَّ ٱهْتَدَىٰ - then committed himself to guidance. The word ثُمَّ indicates this is an ongoing state. It's not enough to repent, believe, and do one good deed. After all that, there must be continuous commitment to staying on the path of guidance.

This progression is beautiful: turn away from wrong, turn toward faith, act on that faith, then persist in guidance. This is the path to divine forgiveness, and it's available even to those who have incurred Allah's ﷻ anger if they genuinely traverse these stages.

## Ayahs 83-84

وَمَآ أَعْجَلَكَ عَن قَوْمِكَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ

قَالَ هُمْ أُو۟لَآءِ عَلَىٰٓ أَثَرِى وَعَجِلْتُ إِلَيْكَ رَبِّ لِتَرْضَىٰ

The scene shifts dramatically. Bani Israel has been rescued and made a covenant with Allah ﷻ. Now they're migrating—hundreds of thousands of them—with Musa (AS) as their leader, heading toward the mountain where Allah ﷻ will speak with him again. 

Musa (AS) has conversed with Allah ﷻ before, and he cannot wait for another opportunity. His eagerness gets the better of him—he rushes ahead on his mount while the massive caravan moves slowly behind. He arrives at the mountain early.

Allah ﷻ asks: "What rushed you away from your nation, O Musa?" The question itself is significant. Did Musa (AS) do something wrong? The tone suggests gentle admonishment. In the work of prophets, rushing is typically not acceptable. The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ was told لَا تَعْجَلْ بِالْقُرْآنِ (don't rush with the Quran). He was instructed فَلَا تَسْتَعْجِل لَّهُم (don't rush for their destruction). Prophets must do things precisely on time, not early, not late. So why did Musa (AS) come early?

His response shows his nervousness at being questioned: "Those guys"—and he uses demonstrative pronouns because they're not far, they're right at the base of the mountain, practically on his footsteps. He's trying to minimize his error: "They're right there, not far behind at all. I didn't leave them that far back."

Then he gives his second excuse: وَعَجِلْتُ إِلَيْكَ رَبِّ لِتَرْضَىٰ (I rushed to You, my Master, to make You happy). It's a beautiful, almost childlike response—I came early because I thought You'd be pleased. His love and eagerness for Allah's ﷻ presence is evident, even if the timing wasn't quite right.

translation: And what made you hasten from your people, O Moses?
commentary:
وَمَآ أَعْجَلَكَ عَن قَوْمِكَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ

*And what caused you to hasten ahead of your people, O Musa?*

The narrative now shifts from the confrontation with Fir'awn to another test entirely. Musa (AS) has successfully led the Children of Israel out of Egypt. The sea has been parted, Fir'awn has been destroyed and divine victory has been granted. Yet the greatest challenges often come after success rather than before it.

Allah ﷻ asks Musa (AS) why he rushed ahead of his people. Of course Allah already knows the answer. The purpose of the question is educational. It draws attention to Musa's state of mind and prepares the listener for what follows.

The word أَعْجَلَكَ comes from haste and eagerness. Not every form of haste is blameworthy. Sometimes a person rushes because of excitement, love and longing. Musa (AS) was eager to meet Allah ﷻ and receive further guidance. His haste was motivated by devotion, not negligence.

This ayah reminds us that even righteous intentions must be accompanied by wisdom and balance. Leadership requires not only moving toward noble goals but also ensuring that one's people are not left vulnerable in the process. The coming events will demonstrate why this lesson is important.

translation: He said, 'They are close upon my tracks, and I hastened to You, my Lord, that You be pleased'
commentary:
قَالَ هُمْ أُو۟لَآءِ عَلَىٰٓ أَثَرِى وَعَجِلْتُ إِلَيْكَ رَبِّ لِتَرْضَىٰ

*He said: They are right behind me, and I hastened to You, my Lord, so that You may be pleased.*

Musa (AS) immediately explains his reasoning. He has not abandoned his people. They are following closely behind him. He merely moved ahead in order to answer Allah's call more quickly.

The phrase لِتَرْضَىٰ is particularly beautiful. 'So that You may be pleased.' This reveals the sincerity of Musa's heart. His motivation was not personal achievement, status or recognition. He simply desired the pleasure of Allah ﷻ.

Throughout the Quran, the prophets demonstrate this quality repeatedly. Their actions are driven by the desire to earn Allah's approval. This becomes the highest ambition of a believer. Wealth, fame and success are all temporary. Allah's pleasure is permanent.

Yet this ayah also contains a subtle lesson. Good intentions alone do not always guarantee the best outcome. Musa's eagerness was sincere, but events unfolding behind him would show that leaders must remain attentive to the condition of those they guide. The believer therefore combines sincerity with wisdom, devotion with responsibility and zeal with balance.

translation: [Allah] said, "But indeed, We have tried your people after you [departed], and the Samiri has led them astray
commentary:
قَالَ فَإِنَّا قَدْ فَتَنَّا قَوْمَكَ مِنۢ بَعْدِكَ وَأَضَلَّهُمُ ٱلسَّامِرِىُّ

Allah's ﷻ response must have sent Musa's heart plummeting: "We have already put your nation to test after you." The word فَتَنَّا (tested) indicates a serious trial has occurred.

Then: وَأَضَلَّهُمُ ٱلسَّامِرِىُّ (As-Samiri has misled them). Who is this troublemaker As-Samiri? Musa (AS) knows about him, but now learns that he has led the people astray.

Allah ﷻ doesn't elaborate on what As-Samiri did. He simply says the man misguided them, leaving Musa's imagination to run wild. What has he done? What have they done? The ambiguity creates intense anxiety. Musa (AS) is being tested now just as his people are being tested. His eagerness to rush ahead has meant leaving his nation vulnerable, and in his absence, they have fallen into grave error.

translation: So Moses returned to his people, angry and grieved. He said, "O my people, did your Lord not make you a good promise? Then, was the time [of its fulfillment] too long for you, or did you wish that wrath from your Lord descend upon you, so you broke your promise [of obedience] to me
commentary:
فَرَجَعَ مُوسَىٰٓ إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ غَضْبَـٰنَ أَسِفًا ۚ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَلَمْ يَعِدْكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ وَعْدًا حَسَنًا ۚ أَفَطَالَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْعَهْدُ أَمْ أَرَدتُّمْ أَن يَحِلَّ عَلَيْكُمْ غَضَبٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ فَأَخْلَفْتُم مَّوْعِدِى

Musa (AS) descends the mountain in a state of intense emotion: غَضْبَـٰنَ أَسِفًا (full of anger and sorrow). The word أَسِفًا means grief, sorrow, regret—he's angry at them but also sorrowful over his own decision to rush ahead. Why did he go early? What has his eagerness cost his people?

His confrontation with them bursts forth: "O my nation, didn't your Master promise you a beautiful promise?" The promise was clear, the covenant was established. Why did they break it?

Then he asks two diagnostic questions: "Has it been so long that you promised that you can't even remember?" Perhaps the time elapsed made them forget or become impatient—has the waiting made the promise seem distant and unreal?

"Or was it that you really wanted Allah's ﷻ anger to be unleashed upon you?" This is sharper—are you actually so foolish that you deliberately sought divine wrath? "Is that why you violated my promise?" The promise was simple: don't do anything until I return. What happened?

His questioning reveals his bewilderment. These are the people who witnessed the parting of the sea, who ate miraculous provisions, who saw Fir'awn drown. How could they fall so quickly?

translation: They said, "We did not break our promise to you by our will, but we were made to carry burdens from the ornaments of the people [of Pharaoh], so we threw them [into the fire], and thus did the Samiri throw
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ مَآ أَخْلَفْنَا مَوْعِدَكَ بِمَلْكِنَا وَلَـٰكِنَّا حُمِّلْنَآ أَوْزَارًا مِّن زِينَةِ ٱلْقَوْمِ فَقَذَفْنَـٰهَا فَكَذَٰلِكَ أَلْقَى ٱلسَّامِرِىُّ

Their response begins with a denial of intentional wrongdoing: "We didn't break our promise with you بِمَلْكِنَا (by our own authority/intention)." The word مَلْك refers to control, authority, intentional decision-making. They're saying, "This wasn't a deliberate choice on our part. We didn't have control over this situation."

Then they explain what happened, and here we must understand the background. When Bani Israel escaped Egypt in the night, they were known among the Egyptians as the honest people. Egyptians used to entrust their jewelry to them for safekeeping, like safety deposit boxes. When the escape was planned, they couldn't return all that jewelry without alerting the Egyptians to their departure. So they fled with it.

Now, carrying this jewelry, they began to feel guilty. This tells us something important: they still had conscience, they still felt remorse. But here's the danger—good intentions can be manipulated without proper knowledge, without revelation.

They explain: حُمِّلْنَآ أَوْزَارًا مِّن زِينَةِ ٱلْقَوْمِ (we were carrying burdens from the adornments of the people). The word أَوْزَار  means burdens, but also has connotations of sin—these ornaments felt like sinful weight.

"So we threw it away"—فَقَذَفْنَـٰهَا. Here's where As-Samiri's manipulation becomes clear. He saw their guilt and exploited it. He told them they needed to dispose of the jewelry because it was a curse—they didn't own it, it was given in trust, and holding onto it would bring divine punishment. His solution? Burn it all, melt it down.

Everyone started throwing their jewelry into a fire to "purify" themselves of this burden. فَكَذَٰلِكَ أَلْقَى ٱلسَّامِرِىُّ (and that's how As-Samiri threw). As-Samiri threw his jewelry too, but he was the one orchestrating the entire plan. He molded the melted gold into the shape of a calf, engineered in such a way that when wind passed through it, it produced a sound like a bull's lowing. The people were amazed—it seemed like a divine sign—and they began worshiping it.

This story reveals something profound about human nature. These people had religious sentiment—they wanted to please Allah ﷻ, they wanted to remember Him, they wanted to be purified. But religious feeling without knowledge, without revelation, can be exploited. People will come along who take advantage of sincere religious sentiments and turn them into an industry, a business.

Why do people follow such manipulation? Because false religion is easier. True religion has expectations—Allah ﷻ wants you to pray, to avoid haram, to discipline yourself. But an idol has no expectations. The person selling false religion tells you: "Just do this simple ritual, and all your sins are forgiven, Paradise is guaranteed, your problems are solved." It requires tribute to the idol but no change to your lifestyle. People naturally incline toward interpretations of religion that promise to remove all problems without requiring them to change.

The danger is that these corrupted forms don't completely abandon Islam. As-Samiri still says "This is the god of Musa"—he keeps the cover of Islam while selling shirk at its core. Even today, people take the Quran and turn it into an amulet for protection, as if the physical pages have power rather than understanding that Allah ﷻ alone protects. They hang it in cars thinking it's a safety device, wear Quranic verses as bulletproof vests, pay for blessed amulets that promise miracles. This is the modern manifestation of As-Samiri's deception.

The lesson stands eternal: sincere religious feeling must be coupled with knowledge and proper understanding of revelation. Without that, even the most well-intentioned believers can be led astray by those who exploit their faith for personal gain. The Bani Israel's mistake wasn't lack of faith—it was lack of patience with revelation and lack of deep understanding. When we fail to truly study and comprehend our Book, we become vulnerable to every As-Samiri who comes along promising easy solutions to complex spiritual questions.

translation: And he extracted for them [the statue of] a calf which had a lowing sound, and they said, "This is your god and the god of Moses, but he forgot
commentary:
فَأَخْرَجَ لَهُمْ عِجْلًا جَسَدًا لَّهُۥ خُوَارٌ فَقَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَآ إِلَـٰهُكُمْ وَإِلَـٰهُ مُوسَىٰ فَنَسِىَ

As-Samiri had manipulated the Israelites into throwing away the jewelry they had borrowed from the Egyptians. After collecting all this gold, he molded it into the shape of a calf. Allah ﷻ says, "Then he extracted for them a calf—a body."

The word جَسَدًا  is one of three Arabic terms for "body," but it carries a specific meaning: a body emptied from the inside, or one without blood flowing through it. This detail reveals the hollow nature of this idol from the very beginning.

The ayah continues: لَّهُۥ خُوَارٌ —"which had a mooing sound in it." When the people heard this sound, they experienced what they perceived as a spiritual encounter. They declared, "This is your god and the god of Musa." Then comes the phrase فَنَسِىَ —"he forgot."

Who forgot? This could refer to Musa (AS), suggesting he forgot where to find his Lord. More likely, it refers to As-Samiri himself, who forgot all the teachings he had acquired from the prophets despite witnessing the miracles of Allah ﷻ firsthand.

translation: Did they not see that it could not return to them any speech and that it did not possess for them any harm or benefit
commentary:
أَفَلَا يَرَوْنَ أَلَّا يَرْجِعُ إِلَيْهِمْ قَوْلًا وَلَا يَمْلِكُ لَهُمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا

Allah ﷻ poses a devastating question: "Don't they see that it doesn't respond to their word—not with a single word?" The phrase أَلَّا يَرْجِعُ إِلَيْهِمْ قَوْلًا  can be understood two ways. Either the idol cannot respond to them with any word, or it cannot even respond to a single word directed at it. Either way, there is no real communication happening.

This is profoundly absurd. The people would approach this idol, ask questions about their lives—should I marry this person, what should I do about this situation—and then hear the monotonous mooing sound. From that single noise, they would construct elaborate interpretations, reading into it whatever answer they wanted to hear.

The ayah continues: وَلَا يَمْلِكُ لَهُمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا —"and it has no authority to bring about any harm or any benefit for them." Notice that harm is mentioned before benefit. This sequence is significant because people who join these cultish devotions often remain trapped by fear—fear of the idol's curse, fear of what might happen if they leave. The idol must be appeased and shown respect, or else. By mentioning harm first, Allah ﷻ addresses this psychological prison.

What's particularly striking is the use of present tense: يَرَوْنَ. This story took place in the distant past with Musa (AS) and the Israelites, yet Allah ﷻ uses the present tense. Why? Because there are actually two parallel stories unfolding. There's the historical account of As-Samiri, the golden calf, and the Israelites. And there's the contemporary reality of the Prophet ﷺ, the Sahaba, and the idolaters of Makkah. The Quran frequently does this—it pulls out of the historical narrative to make a direct comment about present reality. The message to the Arabs was clear: You're doing exactly the same foolish thing. Don't you see?

translation: And Aaron had already told them before [the return of Moses], "O my people, you are only being tested by it, and indeed, your Lord is the Most Merciful, so follow me and obey my order
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ قَالَ لَهُمْ هَـٰرُونُ مِن قَبْلُ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنَّمَا فُتِنتُم بِهِۦ ۖ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ فَٱتَّبِعُونِى وَأَطِيعُوٓا۟ أَمْرِى

Before we learn about Musa's (AS) reaction, Allah ﷻ tells us about Harun's (AS) response: "And Harun had certainly said to them before, 'O my people, you're only being tested by it.'"

The timing here is crucial. Musa (AS) had rushed ahead to meet with Allah ﷻ on the mountain. When Allah ﷻ asked him, "What made you come so quickly?" Musa (AS) could literally point down and say, "They're right there. I can still see them." The people weren't far away at all, which means Musa (AS) hadn't been gone for very long. Yet in this brief period, everything fell apart.

This teaches us a profound lesson: building something good takes a long time, but its destruction can happen remarkably quickly. You can see this pattern in protected Islamic environments. Students who have been carefully raised in conservative families, attending Islamic schools where everyone shares the same values—their entire world seems stable. Then one student from a completely different background joins their class, and within a month, you notice changes spreading through the whole group. What took years to build can unravel in weeks.

The mention of Harun (AS) here is deliberate and significant. Later in the passage, we'll see Musa (AS) become angry with his brother, but before we even reach that point, Allah ﷻ comes to Harun's (AS) defense. We're shown that he tried his best to guide the people. This way, we don't automatically assume that Musa's (AS) anger was justified.

Look at how Harun (AS) called them back: إِنَّمَا فُتِنتُم بِهِۦ —"You're just being tested with this. This is a trial." Then he said something crucial: وَإِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ —"And no doubt, your Master is Ar-Rahman, the Extremely Merciful."

Notice that Harun (AS) didn't say "Allah." He specifically said "Ar-Rahman"—the name that emphasizes overwhelming, encompassing mercy. Why? Because one of the things that makes these deviant practices appealing is that people don't want to take responsibility. They perceive Allah ﷻ as the One who gives laws, who punishes, who threatens hellfire. But this golden calf? It doesn't ask them to do anything difficult. Just leave some offerings, show some respect, and you're fine.

These false gods seem merciful while Allah ﷻ seems too authoritative, too demanding. Harun (AS) corrected this fundamental misunderstanding: "You've got it all wrong. You have the wrong picture of Allah ﷻ in your minds, and that's what led you toward shirk."

This is a critical key for calling back any Muslim community that has deteriorated or deviated from the original teachings. When innovation enters the religion, especially regarding beliefs, the way to call people back is actually to emphasize the excessively merciful nature of Allah ﷻ, not just His authority.

Consider a practical example. You have Muslim friends who've gotten involved in practices where they visit someone considered exceptionally holy—a person they believe can grant wishes or provide blessings. They sit at this person's feet, avoid eye contact, leave money that he doesn't even acknowledge, and he sits there pretending to engage in remembrance while people line up for his prayers and potions.

When you encounter Muslims educated in proper Islamic knowledge, people sometimes react defensively. They see the religiously educated as strict, always talking about halal and haram, making everything forbidden. Life seems so hard when you take Islam seriously, they think. They develop labels: "orthodox," "conservative," "restrictive." They pass judgment based on appearances and limited understanding.

This is why knowledgeable people must undertake a campaign to introduce others to the merciful Allah ﷻ. These misconceptions don't form in a vacuum—they develop because people see Muslims who appear religious but harsh, and they assume that's what Islam demands. They need to be introduced to Ar-Rahman first.

Notice the sequence in Harun's (AS) call: first he introduced them to Ar-Rahman, then he said فَٱتَّبِعُونِى —"so follow me"—and finally وَأَطِيعُوٓا۟ أَمْرِى —"and obey my command." He didn't demand obedience first. He didn't lead with authority. He led with mercy, then invited them to follow, and only then mentioned obedience.

translation: They said, "We will never cease being devoted to the calf until Moses returns to us
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ لَن نَّبْرَحَ عَلَيْهِ عَـٰكِفِينَ حَتَّىٰ يَرْجِعَ إِلَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ

Their response revealed the depth of their corruption: "They said, 'We will not budge from devotion to it until Musa returns to us.'"

The word عَـٰكِفِينَ comes from i'tikaf, which means to stop yourself from doing everything else and confine yourself to worship. In Islamic practice, this refers to secluding oneself in the mosque, cutting off from worldly business, entertainment, and transactions, focusing entirely on worship.
