The Israelites declared they were in this state of devoted concentration before the calf, and they refused to move until Musa (AS) himself returned.

This reveals another major problem in a corrupted nation: the obsession with personalities rather than the message itself. Both Musa (AS) and Harun (AS) were prophets, both were legitimate teachers appointed by Allah ﷻ. But in the people's eyes, the real hero was Musa (AS)—the one with the staff, the one who went up the mountain, the one who challenged Fir'awn, the one who delivered them. So they didn't feel the need to listen to Harun (AS). When Musa (AS) comes back, we'll deal with it then.

This disease persists in Muslim communities today. We become obsessive about one scholar, one speaker, one writer, and say, "If he didn't say it, it can't be true. If she didn't teach it, how can we accept it?" This is a sickness. We are people who should follow ideas, evidence, and truth. Of course we respect and honor our teachers, and we want to learn as much as we can from them. But we must understand that all teachers are human beings who can make mistakes. The only ones who didn't err were the prophets. After them, everyone is susceptible to error. We should take good knowledge wherever we can find it.

## Ayah 92-93

قَالَ يَـٰهَـٰرُونُ مَا مَنَعَكَ إِذْ رَأَيْتَهُمْ ضَلُّوٓا۟ - أَلَّا تَتَّبِعَنِ ۖ أَفَعَصَيْتَ أَمْرِى

When Musa (AS) returned and saw what had happened, the first person he confronted was his brother. Remember what Musa (AS) had said about Harun (AS) up on the mountain? He had praised him before Allah ﷻ, saying, "He's my backbone, he'll take care of things, he speaks better than I do." He had given an entire speech honoring his brother. But now, that same brother faced his anger.

"He said, 'O Harun, what prevented you when you saw them going astray?'" The word مَا مَنَعَكَ  cuts deep—what stopped you? What held you back from doing something?

Then comes the second question: أَلَّا تَتَّبِعَنِ —"Could you not at least follow me?" This could mean two things. Perhaps: "Couldn't you just come up the mountain and let me know? Couldn't you alert me to come back because things were getting out of control?" Or it could mean: "Couldn't you follow my way, my methodology, and enforce what I had established with them?"

The final question stings: أَفَعَصَيْتَ أَمْرِى —"Or is it that you disobeyed my command?"

Scholars note that Musa (AS) was likely the older brother, yet here the younger brother was being reprimanded by someone in a position of prophetic authority. The intensity of the moment was such that Musa (AS) grabbed Harun (AS) physically—a detail we learn from the next ayah.

translation: He said, 'O Harun, what prevented you, when you saw them going astray'
commentary:
قَالَ يَـٰهَـٰرُونُ مَا مَنَعَكَ إِذْ رَأَيْتَهُمْ ضَلُّوٓا۟

*He said: O Harun, what prevented you when you saw them going astray?*

When Musa (AS) returned and discovered the worship of the calf, his reaction was immediate and intense. His anger was not for personal reasons. It was anger for the sake of Allah ﷻ. After witnessing miracles unlike anything seen by previous nations, the Children of Israel had fallen into blatant idolatry.

Musa (AS) turns to his brother Harun (AS), who had been left in charge during his absence. The question reflects both shock and disappointment. How could such a disaster occur while a prophet was among them?

The word ضَلُّوا means they went astray, deviating from the path after guidance had become clear. This makes their error especially serious. Ignorance is one thing; abandoning known truth is another.

The ayah highlights the burden carried by leaders and teachers. When people under their care go astray, they naturally feel responsibility and concern. Musa's question is not merely about blame. It is about understanding how such a deviation was allowed to spread among a people who had witnessed so many signs from Allah ﷻ.

translation: That you did not follow me? Then have you disobeyed my order?
commentary:
أَلَّا تَتَّبِعَنِ ۖ أَفَعَصَيْتَ أَمْرِى

*Why did you not follow me? Have you then disobeyed my command?*

Musa (AS) continues questioning his brother. His words reflect the intensity of the situation. From his perspective, immediate action should have been taken against the growing corruption. The danger was not merely theological error but the possibility that an entire nation might return to shirk after having been rescued by Allah ﷻ.

The phrase أَلَّا تَتَّبِعَنِ can be understood as asking why Harun (AS) did not come after him and inform him of what was happening. If the situation had become uncontrollable, perhaps Musa (AS) could have intervened sooner.

Then comes the sharper question: أَفَعَصَيْتَ أَمْرِى – 'Have you disobeyed my command?' The wording reflects Musa's emotional state at that moment. He is speaking before hearing Harun's explanation.

This interaction teaches an important lesson about human emotion, even among the prophets. Strong emotions can arise when sacred principles are violated. However, the story does not end here. The next verses reveal Harun's response and demonstrate that situations are often more complex than they initially appear. Before judgment is made, explanation must be heard. Before conclusions are reached, the full picture must be understood.

Thus, the exchange between the two brothers becomes a lesson not only about leadership and responsibility but also about patience, communication and seeking understanding before final judgment.

translation: [Aaron] said, "O son of my mother, do not seize [me] by my beard or by my head. Indeed, I feared that you would say, 'You caused division among the Children of Israel, and you did not observe [or await] my word
commentary:
قَالَ يَبْنَؤُمَّ لَا تَأْخُذْ بِلِحْيَتِى وَلَا بِرَأْسِىٓ ۖ إِنِّى خَشِيتُ أَن تَقُولَ فَرَّقْتَ بَيْنَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَلَمْ تَرْقُبْ قَوْلِى

Harun's (AS) response begins with a tender plea: يَبْنَؤُمَّ —"O son of my mother." This is the classical Arabic way of saying "brother from the same mother"—an expression invoking their shared maternal bond. Why mention their mother? Because Musa (AS) had a particularly close relationship with her. Allah ﷻ had already highlighted as one of His great favors to Musa (AS) that his mother's grief was relieved when he was returned to her. By saying "son of my mother," Harun (AS) was essentially saying, "For our mother's sake, for the one we both love, calm down."

"Don't grab my beard," he said, "and don't grab my head." These are mentioned separately because Musa (AS), in his anger, couldn't contain himself. He had grabbed his brother in frustration, and Harun (AS) had to physically ask him to let go.

Then Harun (AS) explained his reasoning: إِنِّى خَشِيتُ —"I was truly afraid"—أَن تَقُولَ فَرَّقْتَ بَيْنَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ —"that you would say I caused division among the Children of Israel."

This is the heart of Harun's (AS) dilemma. He explained, "If I had given them a forceful sermon, if I had said 'You absolutely must stop this now,' a fight would have broken out. There would have been a riot, a quarrel, complete chaos. And when you came back to find them fighting each other, you would have blamed me for that too. You would have said I failed to watch over your instructions."

What were those instructions? Earlier, Allah ﷻ had told us that Musa (AS) said to Harun (AS): اخْلُفْنِى فِى قَوْمِى وَأَصْلِحْ —"Take my place among my people and make reconciliation." The primary instruction was to maintain peace and reconciliation among the Israelites, who had a tendency toward internal conflict.

The phrase وَلَمْ تَرْقُبْ قَوْلِى  means "and you didn't watch over my instructions." Harun (AS) was saying, "You told me to keep them together, to prevent fighting. If I had pushed too hard against the calf worshippers, I would have split the nation in two. Some would have followed me, others would have resisted violently, and you would have returned to find the very division you told me to prevent."

This exchange reveals the impossible position Harun (AS) found himself in—caught between the need to oppose blatant shirk and the command to maintain unity. His choice to speak against the idol worship while avoiding actions that would tear the community apart showed his wisdom, even though in the heat of the moment, his brother couldn't see it.

translation: [Moses] said, "And what is your case, O Samiri
commentary:
قَالَ فَمَا خَطْبُكَ يَـٰسَـٰمِرِىُّ

Before Musa (AS) addresses the people, He turns to As-Samiri and asks: "What's your problem?" The word خَطْب appears in the Quran when something doesn't make sense and you ask someone what's going on. Consider the example of Ibrahim (AS) when angels came to his house. He became worried when they wouldn't eat his food—because in those times, assassins who came to kill wouldn't eat from your food as a matter of honor. When they saw the puzzled look on his face, they explained they came to tell him about his child, he responded with "What's your deal?"—because one angel could have delivered that news. There had to be something more. They eventually revealed they came to destroy the nation of Lut (AS).

Similarly, when Musa (AS) arrived at the waters of Madyan and saw young women struggling with their sheep, he asked them: "What's your problem?" Despite his strength—remember his powerful punch—he chose restraint and simply inquired about their situation.

Now Musa (AS) asks As-Samiri the same question: What is your deal?

translation: He said, "I saw what they did not see, so I took a handful [of dust] from the track of the messenger and threw it, and thus did my soul entice me
commentary:
قَالَ بَصُرْتُ بِمَا لَمْ يَبْصُرُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَقَبَضْتُ قَبْضَةً مِّنْ أَثَرِ ٱلرَّسُولِ فَنَبَذْتُهَا وَكَذَٰلِكَ سَوَّلَتْ لِى نَفْسِى

As-Samiri replies: "I saw something they didn't see." The word بَصُرْتُ is different from the common word for seeing. It means to keep a close watch, to have insight, to perceive something with deeper understanding. He claims, "I had an insight, a brilliant idea that they didn't have."

He continues: فَقَبَضْتُ قَبْضَةً مِّنْ أَثَرِ ٱلرَّسُولِ—"I grabbed a handful from the footsteps of the messenger." The word قَبْضَة specifically means a fistful of something grabbed. This phrase has generated scholarly discussion about its meaning.

One popular opinion holds that As-Samiri secretly followed Musa (AS) when Jibreel (AS) came to converse with him. As-Samiri witnessed the angel's footsteps, and some narrations add that vegetation grew wherever Jibreel (AS) stepped. As-Samiri took some soil from these footsteps, believing it had special powers, and threw it onto the calf he had molded, causing it to move and produce the sound of a cow.

However, there are theological problems with this interpretation—particularly the idea that soil from an angel's footsteps could give life. This isn't directly authenticated in the sources.

Imam Al-Qurtubi presents a compelling alternative based on the language of the ayah. He translates it as: "I saw an opportunity they didn't see. I grabbed a fistful from the traces of the messenger"—here using "messenger" sarcastically, as a critical insult. This happens in the Quran where disbelievers use honorific titles mockingly, as if to say "this so-called messenger of yours."

In this interpretation, As-Samiri is saying: "I took some of the teachings of this apparent messenger—the core beliefs about Allah ﷻ and tawhid—and then I threw them away." The word نَبَذْتُهَا means to throw something you consider worthless. He discarded the foundational teachings and introduced his own innovations.

وَكَذَٰلِكَ سَوَّلَتْ لِى نَفْسِى—"And that's what became convenient to my own self." He felt no guilt introducing his own ideas. He took advantage of the Israelites' request for something to help them concentrate in worship—the same complaint many Muslims have today about distraction in salah.

The Israelites had asked for an idol to help them focus, pointing out that people of other faiths seem to have greater concentration. As-Samiri exploited this weakness, suggesting that Musa (AS) meant well but forgot to mention that using an aid for concentration was acceptable. He twisted the original teachings into a reinterpretation that led to shirk.

This pattern remains relevant today. The worst deviations in Islam are those that twist core beliefs—attacking tawhid, distorting what we believe about the messengers, or corrupting our understanding of the Quran. The people who spread such corruption are a disease to the community.

translation: [Moses] said, "Then go. And indeed, it is [decreed] for you in [this] life to say, 'No contact.' And indeed, you have an appointment [in the Hereafter] you will not fail to keep. And look at your 'god' to which you remained devoted. We will surely burn it and blow it into the sea with a blast
commentary:
قَالَ فَٱذْهَبْ فَإِنَّ لَكَ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ أَن تَقُولَ لَا مِسَاسَ ۖ وَإِنَّ لَكَ مَوْعِدًا لَّن تُخْلَفَهُۥ ۖ وَٱنظُرْ إِلَىٰٓ إِلَـٰهِكَ ٱلَّذِى ظَلْتَ عَلَيْهِ عَاكِفًا ۖ لَّنُحَرِّقَنَّهُۥ ثُمَّ لَنَنسِفَنَّهُۥ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ نَسْفًا

Musa (AS) pronounces judgment: فَٱذْهَبْ—"Leave, go." As-Samiri is made an outcast, expelled from the community. Throughout his life, he will go around saying لَا مِسَاسَ—"No touch at all." The word مِسَاسَ comes from mass, meaning touch, with the shadda removed to emphasize even the slightest contact. Some scholars explain that He was afflicted with a disease so severe that if anyone came near him, both he and they would spike a fever. He became completely cut off from all people.

This punishment teaches us that those who introduce such corruption into the religion are a disease, a danger to others. They must be boycotted and cut off. The community must protect itself from their influence.

وَإِنَّ لَكَ مَوْعِدًا لَّن تُخْلَفَهُۥ—"And you have a promise that won't be broken." This could mean the deadline of death—this isolation will last until you die and won't change. It also refers to the Day of Judgment, when As-Samiri has an appointment with Allah ﷻ for a punishment that will not be avoided.

وَٱنظُرْ إِلَىٰٓ إِلَـٰهِكَ—"Now look at your god"—the calf that he made, the one he remained devoted to with such concentration. لَّنُحَرِّقَنَّهُۥ—"We will burn it to the ground." But there's a challenge: the calf was made of gold, and burning gold only melts it. So Allah ﷻ continues: ثُمَّ لَنَنسِفَنَّهُۥ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ نَسْفًا—"Then we will tear it into fragments and scatter it in the river."

The word تَحْرِيق means to burn something until it reduces to ash or small pieces. First, burn it. Then tear the gold into small fragments. Finally, throw these pieces into the river, drowning any trace of this false god.

Interestingly, some Muslim scholars in India noted parallels between this incident and certain Hindu traditions. The upper-class Brahmins arrived in India roughly around the same time as the Exodus, corresponding to when some tribes of Israel were lost. These traditions hold the cow—especially the yellow or golden cow—as sacred. Yellow and gold are their sacred colors. They have untouchable castes whom no one may touch. They burn offerings, turn them to ash, and throw them in the river. Some scholars have speculated whether one of the lost tribes could have influenced these traditions, though Allah ﷻ knows best.

translation: Your god is only Allah, except for whom there is no deity. He has encompassed all things in knowledge
commentary:
إِنَّمَآ إِلَـٰهُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ وَسِعَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ عِلْمًا

After destroying the false idol, Musa (AS) delivers the foundational truth: إِنَّمَآ إِلَـٰهُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ—"The only One worthy of worship and obedience for you is Allah ﷻ." The particle إِنَّمَآ emphasizes exclusivity and limitation: there is no other.

ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ—"The One besides whom there is no deity." No one else deserves worship or obedience in any way, shape, or form except Him.

وَسِعَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ عِلْمًا—"He encompasses everything in knowledge." His knowledge is complete, comprehensive, and all-encompassing. Nothing escapes His awareness. This stands in stark contrast to the lifeless idol they had worshiped—a creation with no knowledge, no power, no awareness.

translation: Thus, [O Muhammad], We relate to you from the news of what has preceded. And We have certainly given you from Us the Qur'an
commentary:
كَذَٰلِكَ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ مَا قَدْ سَبَقَ ۚ وَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكَ مِن لَّدُنَّا ذِكْرًا

Now Allah ﷻ addresses His Messenger Muhammad ﷺ directly: كَذَٰلِكَ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ مَا قَدْ سَبَقَ—"That is how We narrate to you from some of the accounts, some of the news events of what has already occurred." These are historical narratives with profound lessons.

وَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكَ مِن لَّدُنَّا ذِكْرًا—"And We have given you, especially from Our own self, a powerful remembrance." The word لَّدُنَّا emphasizes "from Us"—directly from Allah ﷻ. The ذِكْر refers to the Quran, a reminder, a remembrance, a preservation of truth. This is the revelation given specifically to the Prophet ﷺ.

translation: Whoever turns away from it - then indeed, he will bear on the Day of Resurrection a burden
commentary:
مَّنْ أَعْرَضَ عَنْهُ فَإِنَّهُۥ يَحْمِلُ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ وِزْرًا

مَّنْ أَعْرَضَ عَنْهُ—"Whoever turns away from it." The word أَعْرَضَ doesn't simply mean to ignore. It means something is right there in front of you, but you deliberately take a turn and avoid it. You go around the other block to escape it. You consciously choose to stay away from this remembrance.

فَإِنَّهُۥ يَحْمِلُ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ وِزْرًا—"In fact, that person is going to be carrying on the Day of Judgment a huge burden, a huge responsibility." The وِزْر isn't just the burden of sins committed. It's the load, the burden of avoiding—of refusing to carry the burden of revelation in this life.

There's a beautiful contrast here. In this life, carrying revelation is like carrying a burden. We even pray: رَبَّنَا وَلَا تُحَمِّلْنَا مَا لَا طَاقَةَ لَنَا بِهِ—"Our Lord, don't load onto us a burden we're not able to carry." (2:286) Revelation is a responsibility we must take care of, protect, and implement.

But the person who avoided that burden in this life—who refused to carry the Quran—will be the one carrying a crushing burden on the Day of Judgment.

translation: [Abiding] eternally therein, and evil it is for them on the Day of Resurrection as a load
commentary:
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهِ ۖ وَسَآءَ لَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ حِمْلًا

خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهِ—"They will remain permanently in that burden." Buried under it. Never escaping it. The word خَـٰلِدِينَ emphasizes eternity, permanence without end.

وَسَآءَ لَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ حِمْلًا—"And how horrible on the Day of Judgment is their ultimate load." Notice Allah ﷻ now uses the word حِمْل instead of وِزْر. While wizr means burden, himl specifically refers to the maximum load possible—the absolute most that can be carried. It's the aftermath of bearing the most you can possibly bear.

How horrible is that Day of Judgment when the ultimate load keeps getting piled on top of them, increasing until they can't take anymore. But unlike in this world where a crushing weight would kill, in the Hereafter, the one being crushed doesn't die and doesn't truly live. That burden increases until the person is crushed, then He is brought back, and it increases again, crushing him again. The cycle continues—the load keeps increasing, over and over.

This is the fate of those who deliberately avoided carrying the light burden of revelation in this life—the burden that would have guided them, protected them, and brought them peace. Instead, they will carry an unbearable load for eternity.

translation: The Day the Horn will be blown. And We will gather the criminals, that Day, blue-eyed
commentary:
يَوْمَ يُنفَخُ فِى ٱلصُّورِ ۚ وَنَحْشُرُ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ يَوْمَئِذٍ زُرْقًا

The Day of Judgment is introduced with the powerful image of the trumpet being blown. Allah ﷻ says, "The day on which it will be blown into the trumpet, and We will gather the criminals on that day, turned blue." The word زُرْقًا describes a striking visual transformation that will overtake the disbelievers on that terrifying day.

The meaning of zurqa has been understood in multiple ways by scholars. Some explain it as describing the color of their eyes turning blue, an expression used in classical Arabic poetry to convey extreme terror. When someone is so frightened that they see death itself, their eyes lose their natural color. Others interpret it as the overall look of death that will be evident on their faces. This is the state in which the criminals will be gathered—marked by visible terror and the appearance of those who have lost all hope.

translation: They will murmur among themselves, "You remained not but ten [days in the world]
commentary:
يَتَخَـٰفَتُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ إِن لَّبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا عَشْرًا

On that Day, these same people who were gathered in a state of terror will be whispering quietly among themselves. The verb يَتَخَافَتُونَ  indicates hushed, fearful conversation. In their distress, they will try to make sense of their situation by discussing how long they remained in the worldly life.

Their conclusion is striking in its brevity: "You remained only ten days." After an entire lifetime on Earth—decades of pursuits, relationships, achievements and failures—they can only recall what feels like a week and a half. This compression of time reveals a profound truth about the transient nature of worldly life. When faced with the eternal reality of the Hereafter, our entire existence in this world will seem like the briefest of moments.

translation: We are most knowing of what they say when the best of them in manner will say, "You remained not but one day
commentary:
نَّحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَقُولُونَ إِذْ يَقُولُ أَمْثَلُهُمْ طَرِيقَةً إِن لَّبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا يَوْمًا

Allah ﷻ responds to their whispered conversation with perfect knowledge: "We know best what they will be saying." He then highlights a particularly telling moment when "the most exemplary of them in lifestyle" will declare, "You remained only a day."

This ayah contains a powerful irony that connects back to earlier passages in the surah. Previously, we learned that Fir'awn told his people that Musa and Harun (AS) wanted to destroy their "exemplary lifestyle" (طريقة مثلى). Now, on Judgment Day, it is "the most exemplary of them in lifestyle" (أَمْثَلُهُمْ طَرِيقَةً) who gives the longest estimate of how much time they spent on Earth—just one day.

This is a devastating commentary on worldly knowledge and status. Those who held the highest positions, who were considered the most cultured and knowledgeable in this life, will prove to be the most ignorant on the Day of Judgment. The ones who thought they knew everything will have nothing of value to say. Their prestigious worldly status will not translate into any wisdom or benefit in the Hereafter.

translation: And they ask you about the mountains, so say, "My Lord will blow them away with a blast
commentary:
وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْجِبَالِ فَقُلْ يَنسِفُهَا رَبِّى نَسْفًا

The scene shifts as Allah ﷻ addresses the Prophet ﷺ: "They ask you about the mountains." This question likely arose from the disbelievers' skepticism about the Day of Judgment. How could the resurrection occur when these massive, immovable mountains exist?

The response is direct and absolute: "Say: My Master will scatter them completely." The verb يَنسِفُهَا means to break something into pieces and scatter it completely. The repetition through the masdar نَسْفًا emphasizes the totality of this destruction. These mountains that seem so permanent and immovable will be utterly demolished and dispersed.

translation: And He will leave the earth a level plain
commentary:
فَيَذَرُهَا قَاعًا صَفْصَفًا

After the mountains are destroyed, Allah ﷻ describes what remains: "And He will leave it as a smooth, level plain." The word قَاعًا refers to a flat, barren ground, while صَفْصَفًا describes smooth rows or levels.

This is the description in Arabic of an endless desert. The imagery evokes sand so smooth you can sink your feet into it. If you were to look at it from an aerial view or stand atop a dune and gaze across it, you would see nothing but rows upon rows of emptiness—smooth, level ground stretching endlessly in every direction.

translation: You will not see therein a depression or an elevation
commentary:
لَّا تَرَىٰ فِيهَا عِوَجًا وَلَآ أَمْتًا

The description becomes even more precise: "You will not see in it any deviation or any elevation." Even the rows of sand that exist in deserts today have ups and downs, rises and falls. But on that Day, the earth will be completely flat—no deviations whatsoever, not even a single dune or rise. It will be perfectly level ground, prepared for the gathering of all humanity.

translation: That Day, everyone will follow [the call of] the Caller [with] no deviation therefrom, and [all] voices will be stilled before the Most Merciful, so you will not hear except a whisper [of footsteps]
commentary:
يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَّبِعُونَ ٱلدَّاعِىَ لَا عِوَجَ لَهُۥ ۖ وَخَشَعَتِ ٱلْأَصْوَاتُ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ فَلَا تَسْمَعُ إِلَّا هَمْسًا

Building on the theme of "no deviations," Allah ﷻ describes the movement of humanity on that Day: "On that Day they will follow the caller, with no deviation from him." When the call is made for humanity to head toward the gathering place at Arafat, every single person will move in perfect unison. People will emerge from their graves all across the planet and head in one direction—not a single person will turn even slightly away.

Think of herding animals: there is always one that strays, one that needs to be guided back. On the Day of Judgment, there will be no straying whatsoever. Every soul will move directly toward the appointed place.

"And all voices will be humbled before Ar-Rahman." The word خَشَعَتِ indicates both fear and being overwhelmed—like when you try to speak but your voice simply will not come out. "So you will hear nothing except a low sound." The word هَمْسًا specifically refers to the soft sound of footsteps.

Imagine this scene: billions upon billions of people, and all you hear is the whisper-soft sound of bare feet moving across that smooth plain. No conversation, no crying out, no protests—just footsteps. The image is both haunting and powerful in its depiction of complete submission before Allah ﷻ.

translation: That Day, no intercession will benefit except [that of] one to whom the Most Merciful has given permission and has accepted his word
commentary:
يَوْمَئِذٍ لَّا تَنفَعُ ٱلشَّفَـٰعَةُ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وَرَضِىَ لَهُۥ قَوْلًا

The ayah addresses a fundamental misunderstanding that led many to shirk: "On that Day, no intercession will benefit except for the one whom Ar-Rahman gives permission, and with whose word He is pleased."

Throughout history, one of the root causes of idol worship has been the belief that certain intermediaries could help people on Judgment Day. People reasoned that while God might punish them, these special beings—close to God—could use their connections to help them out. Allah ﷻ categorically rejects this notion.

On that Day, no one will be able to speak on behalf of anyone else unless two conditions are met. First, Ar-Rahman must grant permission. The very ability to speak on that Day is itself a tremendous mercy. Second, Allah ﷻ must be pleased with that person and what they will say. This is a description of the messengers and the righteous whom Allah ﷻ honors with this role.

The phrase وَرَضِىَ لَهُۥ قَوْلًا has a dual meaning. It means He is pleased with what they will say—their intercession will be truthful and just. It also means He is pleased with the fact that they are the ones speaking. Anyone else attempting to speak would not please Him.

translation: Allah knows what is [presently] before them and what will be after them, but they do not encompass it in knowledge
commentary:
يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِهِۦ عِلْمًا

Allah ﷻ declares His comprehensive knowledge: "He knows what is before them and what is behind them, and they cannot encompass His knowledge." This encompasses both the collective history of humanity and individual human lives.

"What is before them" refers to their future—the unseen events of Judgment Day and what lies ahead. "What is behind them" refers to the ancient past—nations and civilizations that have completely disappeared, whose very traces have been erased from the earth.

But this ayah also speaks to our personal lives. Allah ﷻ knows everything about your past and your future. He knows things about you that you yourself have forgotten. You might look at your record on Judgment Day and think, "I did that? Oh yes, I did." We cannot even remember what we said yesterday. We forget where we put our keys this morning. Our memory is incredibly limited.

Yet Allah ﷻ does not forget a single word, a single action, a single moment. And it is not just you—He maintains this perfect record for every human being who has ever lived. We cannot encompass what He has in terms of knowledge. The gap between His knowledge and ours is infinite.

translation: And [all] faces will be humbled before the Ever-Living, the Sustainer of existence. And he will have failed who carries injustice
commentary:
وَعَنَتِ ٱلْوُجُوهُ لِلْحَىِّ ٱلْقَيُّومِ ۖ وَقَدْ خَابَ مَنْ حَمَلَ ظُلْمًا

The scene intensifies: "And all faces will be humbled before Al-Hayy, Al-Qayyum." The verb عَنَتِ describes faces hanging low in shame and submission. These are not faces held high in defiance or pride—they are bowed down before the Ever-Living, the Sustainer of all existence.

The Divine Names chosen here are significant. Al-Hayy is the source of all life, the Ever-Living who never dies. Al-Qayyum is the One who establishes and maintains everything in existence. Before these attributes of absolute sovereignty and permanence, every face will be lowered.

"And how wretched is the one who carried wrongdoing." The word خَابَ means to be disappointed, humiliated, downtrodden, utterly wretched. Notice that Allah ﷻ says "the one who carried ظُلْمًا"—wrongdoing, even a single act of it. This is not limited to those who committed major sins or lived lives of open rebellion. Even carrying one act of wrongdoing—if it is not forgiven—will make a person wretched on that Day.

translation: But he who does of righteous deeds while he is a believer - he will neither fear injustice nor deprivation
commentary:
وَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَا يَخَافُ ظُلْمًا وَلَا هَضْمًا

After the dire warning comes the promise of hope: "And whoever does any righteous deeds while being a believer, he will fear no injustice nor any deprivation."

The phrase مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ is crucial. The word الصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ already indicates few good deeds—it is the plural form that suggests a limited number. Then Allah ﷻ adds مِنَ ("from"), meaning "anything from the few good deeds." This shows incredible mercy. You do not need mountains of good deeds. Even one sincere good deed, combined with true faith, is enough.

Imagine someone who lived an entire life of sin, then became Muslim near the end of their life and performed just one good deed. If they come before Allah ﷻ with genuine Iman and that single act of righteousness, it is enough. The formula is simple but profound: true conviction in faith, combined with at least some goodness.

Such a person "will fear no injustice nor any deprivation." They will not worry that they will be wrongly punished (dhulm), nor will they fear that their deeds are insufficient (hadm). The word هَضْمًا specifically refers to a shortage or deficiency—like arriving with only a small bag of good deeds while others have truckloads. Allah ﷻ assures the believer: if you have Iman and some goodness, you need not fear that it is not enough.

translation: And thus We have sent it down as an Arabic Qur'an and have diversified therein the warnings that perhaps they will avoid [sin] or it would cause them remembrance
commentary:
وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ قُرْءَانًا عَرَبِيًّا وَصَرَّفْنَا فِيهِ مِنَ ٱلْوَعِيدِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ أَوْ يُحْدِثُ لَهُمْ ذِكْرًا

Allah ﷻ now explains His methodology in the Quran: "And thus We have sent it down as an Arabic Quran, and We have diversified the warnings in it, so that they might become conscious of Allah, or so that it may produce for them a reminder."

The verb صَرَّفْنَا means to turn something in different directions, to present it from multiple angles. The warnings in the Quran come at you from every direction. Allah ﷻ warns you about Judgment Day, about hellfire, about your deeds, about the scales, about accountability—approaching each topic from different perspectives and through different stories.

This is like a teacher who says, "I warned you in every way possible that the exam would be difficult. I told you what your parents would think, what your peers would say, that you would miss a year of school. I warned you from every angle—why couldn't you take me seriously?"

The purpose is twofold. First, "so that they might become conscious of Allah"—so they may develop taqwa and protect themselves. Second, "or so that it may produce for them a reminder." The phrase أَوْ يُحْدِثُ لَهُمْ ذِكْرًا is beautiful. It means the reminder comes to them in a way that feels new, as if they are hearing it for the first time.

This explains why the Quran repeats itself. Allah ﷻ presents the same lessons in different forms so that every time you encounter them with sincerity, you feel rejuvenated. The reminder feels fresh, personalized, as if it is speaking directly to your current situation. This is the miraculous nature of the Quran—though you may have read it a hundred times, each reading can bring a new insight, a new feeling, a reminder that touches your heart as if for the first time.

translation: So high [above all] is Allah, the Sovereign, the Truth. And, [O Muhammad], do not hasten with [recitation of] the Qur'an before its revelation is completed to you, and say, "My Lord, increase me in knowledge
commentary:
فَتَعَـٰلَى ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۗ وَلَا تَعْجَلْ بِٱلْقُرْءَانِ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يُقْضَىٰٓ إِلَيْكَ وَحْيُهُۥ ۖ وَقُل رَّبِّ زِدْنِى عِلْمًا

The ayah begins with exaltation: "So exalted is Allah, the True King." Allah ﷻ mentions His true kingship here because a false king—Fir'awn—has been the central subject of much of this surah.

Then comes instruction to the Prophet ﷺ: "And do not hasten with the Quran before its revelation has been completed to you." This is a remarkable statement given what we have just read about Musa (AS). Earlier in the surah, we learned that Musa (AS) rushed to meet Allah ﷻ on the mountain because of his love and eagerness. Now the Prophet ﷺ is told: do not rush with the Quran before it is revealed to you.

The message is this: Musa (AS) came rushing to Me because he loved Me, and that pleased Me. But I am giving you a different instruction. Do not rush to recite or convey the Quran before I have completed its revelation to you. I will reveal it at the time I choose—at the moment when it is most needed, on the right occasion, for the specific circumstances that require it. Trust in My timing.

"And say: My Master, increase me in knowledge." This dua is profound in its construction. Notice that the Prophet ﷺ is not told to say "increase my knowledge" (زِدْنِي عِلْمًا), but rather "increase me through knowledge" (زِدْنِي عِلْمًا). The difference is subtle but significant.

The prayer is not merely for more information, but for knowledge to make you a better person. Increase me—enhance me, elevate me, transform me—as a result of knowledge. Make knowledge a means of my becoming a better human being, a stronger believer, a more righteous servant.

This is essential because we can accumulate vast amounts of knowledge—we can listen to hundreds of hours of lectures, read countless books—but is that knowledge making us better people? Is it improving our character, strengthening our faith, changing our behavior? Knowledge that does not transform us is knowledge that has failed its purpose. The true value of Islamic knowledge is in how it shapes us as believers.

translation: And We had already taken a promise from Adam before, but he forgot; and We found not in him determination
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ عَهِدْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ ءَادَمَ مِن قَبْلُ فَنَسِىَ وَلَمْ نَجِدْ لَهُۥ عَزْمًا

The narrative now shifts to the story of Adam (AS), which forms the conclusion of this section: "And We had already taken a promise from Adam before, but he forgot, and We did not find in him determination."

The phrase عَهِدْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ indicates a contract or covenant—Allah ﷻ had given Adam (AS) clear instructions. "But he forgot"—فَنَسِىَ. This was not deliberate disobedience but a momentary lapse in memory when he followed the whisper of Shaytan.

The phrase وَلَمْ نَجِدْ لَهُۥ عَزْمًا can be understood in two ways. One interpretation is "We did not find in him the determination" or resolve—meaning Adam (AS) did not have the level of commitment necessary to resist. However, this interpretation seems to diminish the honor of a prophet.

The preferred understanding is: "We did not find in him the determination to sin." He did not have a purposeful intent or firm resolve to disobey Allah ﷻ. His action was not premeditated or committed. It was simply a mistake, a lapse. This interpretation shows Allah's mercy—He is telling us that Adam's error was not an act of rebellion but a moment of forgetfulness. Mistakes happen, and this framing removes blame from Adam (AS).

translation: And [mention] when We said to the angels, "Prostrate to Adam," and they prostrated, except Iblees; he refused
commentary:
وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِـَٔادَمَ فَسَجَدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ أَبَىٰ

The idea that Adam (AS) did not have resolve to sin is reinforced by the next ayah, which recalls his honor: "And when We said to the angels, 'Prostrate to Adam,' and they prostrated, except Iblis—he refused."

This story has appeared multiple times in the Quran, and each time different elements are emphasized. In Surah Al-Baqarah, when this incident occurred, the focus was on what Iblis said to Allah ﷻ in his argument and refusal. Here in Surah Ta-Ha, instead of dwelling on Iblis's dialogue with Allah ﷻ, the ayah immediately moves to what Allah ﷻ said to Adam (AS). The focus shifts to the instruction and warning given to Adam (AS).

translation: So We said, "O Adam, indeed this is an enemy to you and to your wife. Then let him not remove you from Paradise so you would suffer
commentary:
فَقُلْنَا يَـٰٓـَٔادَمُ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا عَدُوٌّ لَّكَ وَلِزَوْجِكَ فَلَا يُخْرِجَنَّكُمَا مِنَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ فَتَشْقَىٰٓ

Allah ﷻ directly addresses Adam (AS): "So We said, 'O Adam, indeed this is an enemy to you and to your wife, so let him not remove you both from Paradise, for then you would be in hardship.'"

The phrasing إِنَّ هَـٰذَا عَدُوٌّ لَّكَ وَلِزَوْجِكَ is instructive. Allah ﷻ does not say "he is an enemy of both of you" collectively. Rather, He says "he is an enemy to you and to your wife"—addressing them separately. This construction carries several benefits.

First, it respects Adam (AS) as the head of the household. Throughout the Quran, when Allah ﷻ gives instructions related to the family dwelling, He addresses Adam (AS) first and includes his wife through him. This is not diminishing the wife's importance but acknowledging the structure of responsibility that Allah ﷻ has established. The man is accountable for guiding and teaching his household.

Second, it establishes Adam (AS) as a teacher in his home. He is being told: this is an enemy to you—and make sure you teach your wife as well. He should not let Shaytan speak only to him or only to her. Both must be aware of the enemy's tactics. This is what the head of a household is meant to be: not just a provider, but a teacher and guide.

Third, the separate mention—"to you and to your wife"—hints at a deeper reality. The way Shaytan attacks men is different from how he attacks women. His approach to each is distinct because the weaknesses and temptations differ. Men face certain vulnerabilities that women may not face as strongly, and vice versa. Shaytan is strategic, coming to each person through their particular weak points.

"So let him not remove you both from Paradise, for then you would be in hardship." The consequence of expulsion is described as شَقَاءٌ—exhaustion, difficulty, struggle. This word connects beautifully with the very beginning of the surah, where Allah ﷻ told the Prophet ﷺ: مَا أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لِتَشْقَىٰٓ—"We have not sent down the Quran to you to cause you hardship."

Adam (AS) is warned that leaving Paradise will result in shaqa—exhaustion and toil. The Quran was revealed to guide us back, not to add to our burdens. This closing reference creates a powerful echo, reminding us that Allah's guidance is always mercy, always a path away from hardship and toward peace.

translation: Indeed, it is [promised] for you not to be hungry therein or be unclothed
commentary:
إِنَّ لَكَ أَلَّا تَجُوعَ فِيهَا وَلَا تَعْرَىٰ

Allah ﷻ addresses Adam (AS) directly, establishing the fundamental guarantees of Paradise: "Indeed, it is [promised] for you not to be hungry therein or be unclothed." The word لَا تَعْرَىٰ  speaks to protection from nakedness, indicating that one of the joys of Paradise is the provision of clothing itself. This becomes significant when we understand that the first sign Adam (AS) was no longer qualified to remain in Paradise was the removal of clothing. 

The emphasis on clothing reveals a profound spiritual principle. In this world, those who deliberately reject the values of Paradise through immodesty signal their disconnect from its reality. When we examine the Quran's treatment of clothing, modesty and shame, we discover this is a major theme that appears long before the specific rulings about dress were revealed. The conversation about modesty in Islam encompasses far more than physical covering—it involves psychology, social interaction, speech, behavior with the eyes and tongue, and how families conduct themselves. All of these elements work together as part of a comprehensive understanding of حياء, often translated as modesty or shame, but encompassing a deeper sense of protective dignity before Allah ﷻ.

translation: And indeed, you will not be thirsty therein or be hot from the sun
commentary:
وَأَنَّكَ لَا تَظْمَؤُا۟ فِيهَا وَلَا تَضْحَىٰ

The guarantees continue: "And indeed, you will not be thirsty therein or be hot from the sun." Allah ﷻ removes from Paradise the fundamental struggles of human survival—hunger, nakedness, thirst and exposure to the elements. These four negations establish Paradise as a realm where basic needs are perpetually satisfied without effort or anxiety.

translation: Then Satan whispered to him; he said, "O Adam, shall I direct you to the tree of eternity and possession that will not deteriorate
commentary:
فَوَسْوَسَ إِلَيْهِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ قَالَ يَـٰٓـَٔادَمُ هَلْ أَدُلُّكَ عَلَىٰ شَجَرَةِ ٱلْخُلْدِ وَمُلْكٍ لَّا يَبْلَىٰ

The word وَسْوَسَ indicates repeated whispering—Shaytan didn't approach Adam (AS) just once. He persisted, returning again and again with his deception. His approach was calculated: "O Adam, shall I direct you to the tree of eternity and possession that will not deteriorate?" 

Shaytan's strategy targeted a fundamental truth—Adam (AS) was not created to remain in Paradise permanently in his current state. He would age. The only path to permanent residence, Shaytan suggested deceptively, was through this forbidden tree. Notice how he frames it as شَجَرَةِ ٱلْخُلْدِ (the tree of eternity) and مُلْكٍ لَّا يَبْلَىٰ (a kingdom that never decays). The temptation wasn't merely about eating fruit—it was about the desire for permanence, for a state that wouldn't diminish with time.

translation: And Adam and his wife ate of it, and their private parts became apparent to them, and they began to fasten over themselves from the leaves of Paradise. And Adam disobeyed his Lord and erred
commentary:
فَأَكَلَا مِنْهَا فَبَدَتْ لَهُمَا سَوْءَٰتُهُمَا وَطَفِقَا يَخْصِفَانِ عَلَيْهِمَا مِن وَرَقِ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۚ وَعَصَىٰٓ ءَادَمُ رَبَّهُۥ فَغَوَىٰ

"And Adam and his wife ate of it, and their private parts became apparent to them, and they began to fasten over themselves from the leaves of Paradise." Notice the dual form فَأَكَلَا—they both ate. Initially, the teaching was given to Adam (AS) who was responsible for conveying it to his spouse. But once she learned the command, they became equally responsible. This is why Allah ﷻ uses the dual form here—both bear responsibility for the action.

The immediate consequence was that their سَوْءَات—their private parts, their shame—became exposed to them. The word سَوْءَة comes from the root meaning ugliness, shame and exposure. There's a spiritual ugliness that accompanies this physical exposure. The verb طَفِقَا indicates they began obsessively, continuously, desperately stitching leaves together to cover themselves, trying to restore what had been lost.

Then comes the stark conclusion: وَعَصَىٰٓ ءَادَمُ رَبَّهُۥ فَغَوَىٰ—"And Adam disobeyed his Lord and erred." After establishing their equal participation, the ayah singles out Adam (AS) for the disobedience. This isn't the perspective of Christianity, which made Eve the embodiment of evil and source of all sin. Islam maintains that both shared responsibility for the action itself, but as the head of the household, as the one who received the original instruction, Adam (AS) bore the ultimate responsibility. If anyone should have stopped the other, it was him. This is the Islamic perspective on leadership and accountability within the family structure.

The word غَوَىٰ means to be far off the path, so far that you don't even know how to return. It describes a state of complete disorientation.

translation: Then his Lord chose him and turned to him in forgiveness and guided [him]
commentary:
ثُمَّ ٱجْتَبَـٰهُ رَبُّهُۥ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدَىٰ

"Then his Lord chose him and turned to him in forgiveness and guided [him]." The particle ثُمَّ indicates a significant passage of time. This wasn't immediate forgiveness. Adam (AS) remained in that state of guilt and sorrow for a period before Allah ﷻ chose him. The verb ٱجْتَبَىٰ specifically means to choose someone because you see good in them. It wasn't that Allah ﷻ chose Adam (AS) because he was the only human available—He chose him because He saw inherent good in him worthy of selection.

The sequence is beautiful: Allah ﷻ chose him, then accepted his repentance, then guided him. The guidance that follows repentance is the most precious gift—it ensures you don't fall into the same error again.

translation: [Allah] said, "Descend from Paradise - all, [your descendants] being enemies to one another. And if there should come to you guidance from Me - then whoever follows My guidance will neither go astray [in the world] nor suffer [in the Hereafter]
commentary:
قَالَ ٱهْبِطَا مِنْهَا جَمِيعًۢا ۖ بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ ۖ فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّى هُدًى فَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ هُدَاىَ فَلَا يَضِلُّ وَلَا يَشْقَىٰ

Allah ﷻ commands both Adam and his spouse to descend together: "Descend from it, all of you, being enemies to one another." This enmity isn't just between humanity and Shaytan—it includes the potential for enmity between men and women, between spouses themselves, and among all of humanity. This is the reality of earthly existence.

But immediately after establishing this challenging reality, Allah ﷻ provides the solution: "And when guidance comes to you from Me, whoever follows My guidance will neither go astray nor suffer." This connects directly back to the beginning of the Surah where Allah ﷻ told the Prophet ﷺ, "We have not sent down to you the Quran that you be distressed" (20:2). The Quran wasn't revealed to make life harder or to burden anyone. Following divine guidance ensures you won't be misled (يَضِلُّ) and you won't be wretched or fail (يَشْقَىٰ). These are the two guarantees for whoever follows the guidance.

translation: And whoever turns away from My remembrance - indeed, he will have a depressed life, and We will gather him on the Day of Resurrection blind
commentary:
وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَن ذِكْرِى فَإِنَّ لَهُۥ مَعِيشَةً ضَنكًا وَنَحْشُرُهُۥ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ أَعْمَىٰ

"And whoever turns away from My remembrance—indeed, he will have a depressed life, and We will gather him on the Day of Resurrection blind." The word أَعْرَضَ means to deliberately turn away, to consciously ignore. This isn't accidental neglect—it's willful rejection of ذِكْر (dhikr), the remembrance of Allah ﷻ.

The consequence is مَعِيشَةً ضَنكًا—a life that is constricted, tight, suffocating. The word ضَنك comes from the root meaning narrowness and difficulty. People imagine those who abandon remembrance of Allah ﷻ live free, unrestricted lives. Allah ﷻ reveals the opposite—their lives become spiritually and psychologically constricted. They may require medications to sleep, suffer from depression and anxiety, need constant distraction through blasting music or entertainment, turn to substances to escape their reality. They're not free—they're trapped in a narrow existence.

The second consequence comes on Judgment Day: resurrection as blind. This connects directly to the state in which they lived—they were already blind to truth, and that blindness becomes their permanent condition.

translation: He will say, "My Lord, why have you raised me blind while I was [once] seeing
commentary:
قَالَ رَبِّ لِمَ حَشَرْتَنِىٓ أَعْمَىٰ وَقَدْ كُنتُ بَصِيرًا

The person protests: "My Lord, why have You raised me blind while I was [once] seeing?" This is the complaint of someone who had physical sight in the world but is raised without it. The irony is profound—they're questioning their physical blindness while remaining oblivious to the spiritual blindness that caused it.

translation: [Allah] will say, "Thus did Our signs come to you, and you forgot them; and thus will you this Day be forgotten
commentary:
قَالَ كَذَٰلِكَ أَتَتْكَ ءَايَـٰتُنَا فَنَسِيتَهَا ۖ وَكَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْيَوْمَ تُنسَىٰ

Allah's response is direct and devastating: "He will say, 'Thus did Our signs come to you, and you forgot them; and thus will you this Day be forgotten.'" The word نَسِيتَ means you forgot them, you ignored them, you abandoned them deliberately. When the Quran was recited, when the call to prayer was made, when reminders came—you turned away. You forgot Allah's signs.

The principle of divine justice is perfect symmetry: just as you forgot the signs of Allah ﷻ, today you are forgotten. This has immediate practical implications. When someone deliberately delays prayer, thinking they'll catch the second rak'ah and it's not a big deal, they're purposely ignoring the reminder. When someone has the opportunity to hear or learn the Quran but chooses entertainment instead, they're deliberately turning away from remembrance.

For Muslims who don't understand Arabic, this presents a specific challenge. Those who comprehend the Arabic of the Quran receive reminders directly in their prayers—each Salah becomes a divine lecture. But those who don't yet understand must compensate. They must actively seek out opportunities to learn the meanings, to hear explanations, to study the message. Working toward understanding the Arabic of the Quran is essential, but until that's achieved, deliberately seeking knowledge through lectures, study circles and authentic sources becomes an obligation. You cannot simply ignore these opportunities and claim you're fulfilling the requirement of being reminded. Listening to Arabic recitation without comprehension provides some reward, but it's not the complete reminder Allah ﷻ intends. True reminder comes through understanding.

translation: And thus do We recompense he who transgressed and did not believe in the signs of his Lord. And the punishment of the Hereafter is more severe and more enduring
commentary:
وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى مَنْ أَسْرَفَ وَلَمْ يُؤْمِنۢ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِۦ ۚ وَلَعَذَابُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَشَدُّ وَأَبْقَىٰٓ

"And thus do We recompense he who transgressed and did not believe in the signs of his Lord. And the punishment of the Hereafter is more severe and more enduring." The word أَسْرَفَ means to go overboard, to exceed all bounds. This describes someone who is excessive in everything—excessive in entertainment, in sleep, in laziness, in gaming, in worldly pursuits. They go overboard in every dimension of life except in their relationship with Allah ﷻ. Despite all this excess, they don't believe in the signs of their Lord.

The punishment of the Hereafter is characterized by two qualities: أَشَدُّ (more severe) and أَبْقَىٰ (more lasting). Whatever suffering exists in this world pales in comparison to what awaits in intensity and duration.

translation: Then, has it not become clear to them how many generations We destroyed before them as they walk among their dwellings? Indeed in that are signs for those of intelligence
commentary:
أَفَلَمْ يَهْدِ لَهُمْ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا قَبْلَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ يَمْشُونَ فِى مَسَـٰكِنِهِمْ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلنُّهَىٰ

"Then, has it not become clear to them how many generations We destroyed before them as they walk among their dwellings? Indeed in that are signs for those of intelligence." This ayah addresses the Quraysh directly, but its message extends to all who witness the ruins of destroyed civilizations. These weren't abstract peoples from distant lands—they lived in the same regions, walked the same paths, inhabited similar homes. The Quraysh would pass by the ruins of Thamud, the destroyed cities of previous nations, seeing remnants of civilizations that once thrived.

The question challenges their perception of safety. When you're walking through a shopping mall, eating ice cream, hanging out with friends in what seems like a perfectly safe environment, what could go wrong? The ruins around them testified that Allah's punishment can descend upon any nation at any time, regardless of how secure they feel. These aren't just historical curiosities—they're living signs for أُو۟لِى ٱلنُّهَىٰ, those who possess sharp intellect that can trace things to their logical conclusions, who can connect cause and effect across time.

translation: And if not for a word that preceded from your Lord, punishment would have been an obligation [due immediately], and [if not for] a specified term [decreed]
commentary:
وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ لَكَانَ لِزَامًا وَأَجَلٌ مُّسَمًّى

"And if not for a word that preceded from your Lord, it would have been an obligation [due immediately], and [if not for] a specified term [decreed]." The word لِزَام comes from a root meaning something that sticks. If Allah ﷻ hadn't already decreed a specific timeframe for judgment, the punishment would have stuck to them immediately, adhered to them without delay. But Allah ﷻ has appointed أَجَلٌ مُّسَمًّى—a specifically named time, the Day of Judgment with its many names and descriptions. Until that appointed time, there's opportunity for repentance and return.

translation: So be patient over what they say and exalt [Allah] with praise of your Lord before the rising of the sun and before its setting; and during periods of the night [exalt Him] and at the ends of the day, that you may be satisfied
commentary:
فَٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ قَبْلَ طُلُوعِ ٱلشَّمْسِ وَقَبْلَ غُرُوبِهَا ۖ وَمِنْ ءَانَآئِ ٱلَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْ وَأَطْرَافَ ٱلنَّهَارِ لَعَلَّكَ تَرْضَىٰ

"So be patient over what they say and exalt [Allah] with praise of your Lord before the rising of the sun and before its setting; and during periods of the night [exalt Him] and at the ends of the day, that you may be satisfied." This ayah prescribes a comprehensive prayer schedule to the Prophet ﷺ, establishing eight distinct times for worship throughout the day and night:

1. Before sunrise (Fajr)
2. After Fajr 
3. Late morning
4. A little before midday
5. At midday (Dhuhr)
6. After midday (Asr)
7. Before sunset (Maghrib)
8. Periods of the night (Isha)

The 24-hour day is divided into roughly eight three-hour segments, creating a rhythm of regular connection with Allah ﷻ. Five of these prayers became mandatory for all Muslims, while three remain optional. This structure ensures that no more than three hours pass without an opportunity to stand before Allah ﷻ.

The conclusion of the ayah reveals the profound purpose: لَعَلَّكَ تَرْضَىٰ—"that you may be satisfied," that you may be pleased. The singular pronoun specifically addresses the Prophet ﷺ. At the beginning of the Surah, we learned that the Mi'raj, the Night Journey and Ascension, was the most pleasing experience of the Prophet's ﷺ life—he spoke directly with Allah ﷻ. That conversation wasn't meant to be a one-time event. Through Salah, Allah ﷻ established the means for the Prophet ﷺ to speak with Him regularly, multiple times throughout each day. The conversation that began in Surah Ta-Ha continues in every prayer. This is how the Prophet ﷺ maintains that state of being pleased—through constant connection with the One who gives him peace.

translation: And do not extend your eyes toward that by which We have given enjoyment to [some] categories of them, [its being but] the splendor of worldly life by which We test them. And the provision of your Lord is better and more enduring
commentary:
وَلَا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلَىٰ مَا مَتَّعْنَا بِهِۦٓ أَزْوَٰجًا مِّنْهُمْ زَهْرَةَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا لِنَفْتِنَهُمْ فِيهِ ۚ وَرِزْقُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقَىٰ

"And do not extend your eyes toward that by which We have given enjoyment to [some] categories of them, [its being but] the splendor of worldly life by which We test them. And the provision of your Lord is better and more enduring." The command وَلَا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ literally means don't let both your eyes stretch toward something. It's a vivid image of longing, of straining to see what others possess.

The word أَزْوَٰج is significant—it refers to pairs, groups, categories of people who are comparable to one another and dependent on each other. Unlike other Arabic words for groups, azwaj specifically describes interconnected power structures: the political elite connected to the economic elite, the intellectual class supporting those in authority, corrupt military maintaining corrupt governments, religious leaders legitimizing kings. These are networks of mutual benefit and protection, maintaining each other's power and status.

Allah ﷻ describes what they possess as زَهْرَةَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا—the flower, the splendor, the glittering decoration of worldly life. Zahrah means attraction, beautiful decor, lights and glamour. It's all surface, all appearance. The purpose? لِنَفْتِنَهُمْ فِيهِ—"that We may test them with it." Their wealth and power is their test, not their blessing.

In contrast, رِزْقُ رَبِّكَ—the provision of your Lord—is خَيْرٌ (better) and أَبْقَىٰ (more lasting). For the Prophet ﷺ specifically, this provision is the Quran itself. That's the real sustenance, the true wealth that surpasses all worldly possessions.

translation: And enjoin prayer upon your family [and people] and be steadfast therein. We ask you not for provision; We provide for you, and the [best] outcome is for [those of] righteousness
commentary:
وَأْمُرْ أَهْلَكَ بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَٱصْطَبِرْ عَلَيْهَا ۖ لَا نَسْـَٔلُكَ رِزْقًا ۖ نَّحْنُ نَرْزُقُكَ ۗ وَٱلْعَـٰقِبَةُ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ

"And enjoin prayer upon your family and be steadfast therein. We ask you not for provision; We provide for you, and the [best] outcome is for [those of] righteousness." This command connects to a recurring theme throughout the Surah. Three situations have now been presented where the head of household takes responsibility for spiritual instruction:

1. Adam (AS) was told, "This is your enemy," and was responsible for teaching his wife
2. Musa (AS) told his family, "Stay here, I'm going to get guidance"  
3. Now the Prophet ﷺ receives the command: teach your family to pray

The verb أْمُرْ is a command—enjoin, command, instruct your family in prayer. Then comes وَٱصْطَبِرْ—remain patient in that instruction. The istif'al form intensifies the meaning: be extremely patient, persistently patient. Your 18-year-old is sleeping through Fajr. You wake them up. "Just five minutes more." No—remain patient in the instruction. Keep waking them. Don't resort to harshness or violence, but persist until they establish the habit. Some fear waking family members because of mood swings or negative reactions. The command doesn't make exceptions for discomfort.

Then comes the powerful reassurance: لَا نَسْـَٔلُكَ رِزْقًا ۖ نَّحْنُ نَرْزُقُكَ—"We don't ask you for provision; We provide for you." Allah ﷻ doesn't gain anything from your prayer. He's not asking you to give Him something—He's giving you something. The prayer was prescribed so that you could be pleased, as the previous ayah stated. This isn't a burden placed on you—it's provision given to you.

وَٱلْعَـٰقِبَةُ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ—"The final outcome belongs to those who protected themselves." Taqwa, consciousness of Allah ﷻ and protection from His displeasure, is what secures a positive end. This is the ultimate success.

translation: And they say, "Why does he not bring us a sign from his Lord?" Has there not come to them evidence of what was in the former scriptures
commentary:
وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْلَا يَأْتِينَا بِـَٔايَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦٓ ۚ أَوَلَمْ تَأْتِهُم بَيِّنَةُ مَا فِى ٱلصُّحُفِ ٱلْأُولَىٰ

"And they say, 'Why does he not bring us a sign from his Lord?' Has there not come to them evidence of what was in the former scriptures?" The disbelievers demand spectacular miracles like those given to Musa (AS)—the staff, the hand, the parting of the sea. Why doesn't Muhammad ﷺ produce such signs?

Allah's response redirects their attention to what they've already received: أَوَلَمْ تَأْتِهُم بَيِّنَةُ—"Has clear evidence not already come to them?" The evidence is مَا فِى ٱلصُّحُفِ ٱلْأُولَىٰ—what was in the earliest scriptures, the former revelations. The Quran contains information that was known only to the scholars of previous nations, details buried in their books, classified information they would never have shared. Yet this unlettered Prophet ﷺ recites it accurately, providing details about previous prophets, ancient nations, theological matters that were hidden. Isn't that miracle enough?

The Quran itself is the standing miracle—not a one-time spectacular display, but an enduring sign that can be examined, studied and verified by every generation.

translation: And if We had destroyed them with a punishment before him, they would have said, "Our Lord, why did You not send to us a messenger so we could have followed Your verses before we were humiliated and disgraced
commentary:
وَلَوْ أَنَّآ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهُم بِعَذَابٍ مِّن قَبْلِهِۦ لَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا لَوْلَآ أَرْسَلْتَ إِلَيْنَا رَسُولًا فَنَتَّبِعَ ءَايَـٰتِكَ مِن قَبْلِ أَن نَّذِلَّ وَنَخْزَىٰ

"And if We had destroyed them with a punishment before it, they would have said, 'Our Lord, why did You not send to us a messenger so we could have followed Your verses before we were humiliated and disgraced?'" This ayah reveals human nature's tendency to make excuses. Allah ﷻ explains that if He had destroyed these people before sending the Prophet ﷺ, they would have protested: "If only You had sent us a messenger! We would have followed Your guidance before we were subdued and humiliated!"

The two words at the end are significant. ذَلَّ means to be overpowered, subdued by a higher force, to lose power completely. خِزْى means to be humiliated publicly, to lose respect and honor in front of others. They claim they would have believed if only they'd been given fair warning—but Allah ﷻ knows they're making excuses. The messenger has come, the clear signs have been presented, yet they still reject. Their protest is hollow.

translation: Say, "Each [of us] is waiting; so wait. For you will know who are the companions of the sound path and who is guided
commentary:
قُلْ كُلٌّ مُّتَرَبِّصٌ فَتَرَبَّصُوا۟ ۖ فَسَتَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلصِّرَٰطِ ٱلسَّوِىِّ وَمَنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ

"Say, 'Each [of us] is waiting; so wait. For you will [soon] know who are the companions of the sound path and who is guided.'" The command to the Prophet ﷺ is to declare: كُلٌّ مُّتَرَبِّصٌ—everyone is sitting around waiting, everyone is in a state of expectation and procrastination. The disbelievers are waiting to see if Islam will fail. The believers are waiting for Allah's promise to be fulfilled. 

فَتَرَبَّصُوا۟—"So go ahead and wait." There's a challenge in these words. Let's see how this plays out. Let's see what happens.

فَسَتَعْلَمُونَ—"Then you will soon know." The future tense carries certainty. The truth will become clear. But what exactly will become clear? Two things:

مَنْ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلصِّرَٰطِ ٱلسَّوِىِّ—who are the companions of the even path, the straight path. The word سَوِىّ comes from the root meaning equally distant from both extremes, right in the middle. It describes something perfectly straight because it's equidistant from the far right and far left, from the top and bottom. This is why Islam is called the middle path—it avoids the extremes of excessive love leading to shirk on one side, and arrogant rejection leading to disbelief on the other. The straight path runs right down the center.

وَمَنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ—and who was truly guided, who was genuinely committed to guidance. Time will reveal who actually walked the straight path and who merely claimed to follow guidance while straying far from it.

With this powerful conclusion, the Surah completes its message. From the opening declaration that the Quran wasn't revealed to cause distress, through the story of Musa (AS) receiving revelation and confronting Pharaoh, through the lesson of Adam (AS) and the consequences of forgetting Allah's signs, to these final commands about prayer, family responsibility and patience—the Surah establishes that divine guidance is the only path to true success, while turning away from it leads to constriction in this life and blindness in the next.
